---
title: "Classifying Music Production Aesthetic from Spotify Audio Features"
author: "Taylor He"
date: "2026-05-20"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: '3'
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    code_folding: hide
    theme: flatly
---

# 1 Preparation
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)
```

```{r libraries}
# Data manipulation
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)

# Visualization
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
library(patchwork)
library(tidytext)

# Machine learning
library(tidymodels)
library(glmnet)
library(ranger)
library(xgboost)
library(vip)
library(SuperLearner)

# Evaluation
library(pROC)
library(yardstick)

# Parallel computation
library(doParallel)
library(parallel)

# Reproducibility
set.seed(2026)
```

---

# 2 Introduction and Intended Use

Music production aesthetic refers to the sonic character of a track shaped by mastering, compression, mixing, and arrangement decisions rather than compositional choices about melody or harmony. Two broad production aesthetics have been identifiable across popular music for at least three decades. A **maximalist** aesthetic is characterized by loudness, density, and dynamic compression, with roots in hard rock, grunge, and the compressed commercial pop production pioneered by producers such as Max Martin in the 1990s and 2000s (Gracyk, 1996; Seabrook, 2015; Walser, 1993). An **alternative** aesthetic, emerging as a counter-movement in independent rock and post-punk production, is characterized by dynamic range, spatial depth, and sonic restraint (Azerrad, 2002; Reynolds, 2006). Neither aesthetic is genre-bound; both have diffused across genre categories as producers moved between musical worlds.

The predictor developed here classifies songs as alternative or maximalist based on Spotify audio features. It could be used by music streaming platforms to organize playlists and recommendation systems by production aesthetic rather than genre, by producers and A&R professionals to identify aesthetic trends across a catalog, or by researchers studying how production practices have changed over time. The predictor takes as input the standard Spotify audio features for any song and outputs a probability of belonging to the maximalist category and a binary classification.

Loudness is deliberately excluded despite being the most theoretically direct correlate of maximalism, because the aesthetic labels used in this study were derived partly from producer communities associated with loudness practices, and including it would introduce circular reasoning into the predictor. The exclusion also serves a constructive purpose by demonstrating that production aesthetic is computationally detectable from audio features beyond its most obvious acoustic correlate.

---

# 3 Study Design for the data and its effects

## 3.1 Primary Dataset

The primary dataset is the Spotify Tracks Dataset, a publicly available collection of approximately 114,000 songs distributed across 114 genres, with roughly 1,000 tracks represented in each genre. The dataset is available through Kaggle and has been used in previous computational music studies (Asmitha & Kavitha, 2026). The dataset spans recordings from the 1960s onward in order to capture the broader history of popular music production, valid and necessary for the prediction task. For each track, Spotify provides twelve audio features that are used as predictors in the classification models. These variables are summarized in Table 1.

| Attribute | Scale | Definition |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Acousticness | 0–1 | Represents the likelihood that the song was recorded solely by acoustic means (as opposed to more electronic/electric means). |
| Danceability | 0–1 | Describes how suitable a track is for dancing. This measure includes tempo, regularity of beat, and beat strength. |
| Energy | 0–1 | A perceptual measure of intensity throughout the track. Think fast, loud, and noisy (i.e., hard rock) more than dance tracks. |
| Instrumentalness | 0–1 | The likelihood that a track is predominantly instrumental. Not necessarily the inverse of speechiness. |
| Key | 0–11 (integers only) | The estimated, overall key of the track, from C through B. Entered as a series of dummy variables in our analyses. |
| Liveness | 0–1 | Detects the presence of a live audience during the recording. Heavily studio-produced tracks score low on this measure. |
| Mode | 0 or 1 | Whether the song is in a minor (0) or major (1) key. |
| Speechiness | 0–1 | Detects the presence of spoken word throughout the track. Sung vocals are not considered spoken word. |
| Tempo | Beats per minute (BPM) | The overall average tempo of a track. |
| Time Signature | Beats per bar/measure | Estimated, overall time signature of a track. 4/4 is the most common time signature by far and is entered as a dummy variable in our analyses. |
| Valence | 0–1 | The musical positiveness of the track. |

: Table 1. The Echo Nest Sonic Features

Kaggle link: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/priyamchoksi/spotify-dataset-114k-songs (The introduction says there are 125 genres in the dataset but that is inaccurate. I only found 114 genres in the actual dataset.)

Together, they provide a standardized numerical representation of musical recordings that can be compared across large collections of songs. Rather than working directly with audio waveforms, the analysis relies on these pre-computed variables as indicators of rhythm, dynamics, timbre, tonality, vocal presence, and other perceptually salient characteristics of recorded music.

These features originated with The Echo Nest, a music intelligence company acquired by Spotify in 2014, and are now integrated into Spotify's audio analysis infrastructure. They are generated algorithmically through a combination of digital signal processing techniques, machine learning models trained on large audio corpora, and statistical analyses of acoustic patterns extracted from recordings (Askin & Mauskapf, 2017; Kim & Askin, 2024). Over the past decade, they have become one of the most widely used feature sets in Music Information Retrieval research and commercial recommendation systems. Their continued use across both academic and industry settings reflects their demonstrated ability to capture musically meaningful variation and support tasks such as genre classification, similarity estimation, playlist generation, and preference prediction.

An additional advantage of these features is their accessibility. Raw audio files and spectrogram representations contain substantially richer information, but their use requires large storage capacity, extensive pre-processing pipelines, and considerably greater computational resources. By contrast, Spotify's audio features are readily available, already structured for statistical analysis, and can be incorporated directly into conventional machine learning workflows. This makes large scale analyses feasible without the computational demands associated with deep learning approaches based on raw audio inputs.

At the same time, the suitability of these features for identifying production aesthetics remains an empirical question. The variables were designed to summarize general musical characteristics rather than to detect distinctions between alternative and maximalist production styles. Many of the differences that define production aesthetic emerge through recording, mixing, mastering, and arrangement decisions that may not be fully captured by summary level audio descriptors. It is therefore possible that representations derived directly from raw audio signals or spectrograms would provide stronger information for this classification task.

The present study thus treats Spotify's audio features as a theoretically plausible and widely validated starting point rather than a definitive representation of production style. Model performance therefore serves not only as an evaluation of the classifiers themselves but also as an indirect assessment of how much information about production aesthetic is contained within this commonly used feature set. Poor performance would suggest that the distinction is not strongly encoded in these variables, whereas strong performance would indicate that production aesthetic leaves detectable traces in the audio characteristics captured by Spotify's analysis framework.

## 3.2 Production Credits and Apple Music Scraping

The aesthetic labels used in this study required song-level production credits, specifically the identities of the producers associated with each track. Production credits were collected from Apple Music, which provides detailed metadata on producers, engineers, and mixers at the individual track level. Because these personnel are directly involved in recording, mixing, and mastering decisions, their credits provide the basis for identifying production aesthetics associated with alternative and maximalist styles (Katz, 2015; Milner, 2010). All songs in the Spotify dataset were successfully matched to corresponding Apple Music entries, allowing production credits to be linked to every track.

Several challenges arose during data collection. Differences in artist and track name formatting across platforms required fuzzy string matching to align records. Some tracks lacked complete production credits because the information was unavailable in Apple Music metadata or because the recordings predated contemporary digital credit practices. Large-scale web-scraping also encountered rate limits and occasional access interruptions, requiring manual verification for a small number of cases.

These issues introduce a limited amount of label uncertainty into the dataset. Missing or incomplete credits are concentrated among older releases and less commercially prominent recordings, where metadata quality is generally lower. Such recordings are also more likely to occupy ambiguous positions between alternative and maximalist production styles. As a result, classification difficulty and label uncertainty are not entirely independent. Any resulting errors are therefore more likely to occur among observations that are substantively difficult to classify rather than among clear examples of either aesthetic category.

## 3.3 Why Binary Labels and the Expert Seed Set

The binary framing of production aesthetic reflects both a historical argument and the structure of the labeling procedure. The maximalist aesthetic is associated with loudness, density, and dynamic compression, while the alternative aesthetic emphasizes dynamic range, spatial separation, and restraint in recording and mixing practices. Although both traditions have earlier roots, they emerged as recognizable and commercially significant production orientations during the late twentieth century. By the 1990s, alternative production had moved beyond its countercultural origins and become an established component of mainstream popular music. The dataset spans recordings from the 1960s onward in order to capture the broader history of popular music production, but the labeling framework reflects the period in which these two aesthetic orientations became sufficiently stable and visible to support systematic classification.

The labels were constructed through a two-stage process combining expert initialization with network analysis. First, an initial set of 30 producers for each aesthetic category was identified through review of music criticism, audio engineering publications, and production scholarship, including sources such as Rolling Stone, Pitchfork, Mix Magazine, and Sound on Sound. Producers were included only when their association with a particular aesthetic was consistently recognized across multiple sources and was not reducible to genre affiliation alone. This produced a seed set of 60 producers and constituted the only stage involving substantive interpretation.

Next, a producer co-credit network was constructed from Apple Music metadata, with connections linking producers who worked on the same track. Louvain community detection was applied to identify densely connected groups of producers by maximizing network modularity. The seed labels anchored the resulting communities and allowed aesthetic assignments to propagate through co-credit relationships to other producers in the network. Each song was then assigned the aesthetic label of its credited producer. The resulting binary classification thus emerged from the structure of the producer network rather than from a predetermined assignment of labels to individual recordings.

## 3.4 Informative Sampling

The Spotify Tracks Dataset provides a large and systematically constructed collection of commercially released recordings suitable for analyzing production aesthetics at scale. It contains approximately 114,000 songs spanning 114 genres, with roughly 1,000 songs sampled per genre. The number of 114 genres reflects a deliberate trade off between coverage and feasibility, as this level of genre granularity already spans the vast majority of stylistic categories represented within modern commercial music. This design is intended to be comprehensive across musical styles represented on Spotify while maintaining balance across genres and ensuring variation in artists, time periods, and popularity levels. In this sense, the dataset prioritizes structured coverage of the platform’s genre space rather than exhaustive enumeration of all possible genre labels. Combined with Spotify’s widely used audio features and production credit data collected from Apple Music, the dataset provides the necessary inputs for both feature based modeling and aesthetic label construction.

The dataset is therefore best understood as an informative sample of Spotify’s commercially released music catalog. The genre based construction ensures broad representation across stylistic categories within the platform, while the inclusion of a popularity measure reflects real streaming activity at the time of collection. However, this also implies that recordings from commercially prominent genres and widely distributed artists are more likely to be included, since these are more strongly represented within Spotify’s catalog and metadata infrastructure. This is consistent with the dataset’s focus on commercially released music with well established metadata coverage rather than an attempt to represent all possible forms of recorded sound.

Within this scope, the dataset is well aligned with the substantive focus of the study. The production aesthetic labels are derived from producer communities and critical discourse that emerge primarily within the same commercial music industry structure captured by Spotify’s catalog. As a result, the sampling frame and the labeling framework operate within a shared institutional context, which supports the validity of the operationalization for studying alternative and maximalist production within popular music.

The primary limitation concerns external generalizability. The reported performance metrics reflect model behavior within a structured and genre balanced sample of Spotify music rather than a random sample of all recordings globally. Applications to musical traditions, production systems, or regional industries that are less fully represented within Spotify’s catalog would require additional validation. Accordingly, the results should be interpreted as evidence for production aesthetic classification within the commercially released popular music domain represented by the dataset.

## 3.5 Anglo-Centric Limitation and Generalizability

A more fundamental limitation of the dataset concerns its geographic and cultural composition. The genre selection and popularity structure underlying the dataset reflect the dominant distribution patterns of the global streaming economy, which are disproportionately concentrated in Anglo American and broader North American and European music industries. The aesthetic categories used in this study, maximalist and alternative, are also derived from concepts developed within Anglophone popular music criticism and production scholarship. Similarly, the seed producers used to initialize the community detection procedure are drawn primarily from this same tradition. As a result, the classifier captures aesthetic distinctions that are meaningful within Anglophone commercial popular music, but these distinctions may not transfer directly to musical traditions organized around different production practices, evaluative standards, and critical vocabularies.

If applied to datasets with substantially greater representation from non Anglophone music traditions, such as Korean popular music, Afrobeats, Latin urban styles, or regional Indian film music, the underlying feature distributions and production conventions may differ in ways that are not well aligned with the labeling structure used here. In such contexts, the model would still produce predictions, but those outputs would reflect the application of an Anglophone derived aesthetic framework rather than a locally grounded set of production categories. The resulting classifications should therefore be interpreted with caution outside of mainstream Anglophone popular music.

Accordingly, any extension of this classifier beyond the domain represented in the training data should involve additional validation against domain specific critical, scholarly, or industry based sources in order to ensure that the inferred aesthetic distinctions remain meaningful within the relevant musical context.

---

# 4 Data Loading and Cleaning

```{r load-data}
df_raw <- read_csv("labeled_dataset.csv")
head(df_raw)
summary(df_raw)

cat("Raw dataset dimensions:", nrow(df_raw), "rows,", ncol(df_raw), "columns\n")
cat("\nColumn names:\n")
print(names(df_raw))
cat("\nLabel distribution (raw):\n")
print(table(df_raw$production_aesthetic))
```

```{r clean-data}
df <- df_raw %>%
  # Drop identifier, metadata, and excluded columns
  select(-any_of(c(
    "...1", "track_id", "artists", "album_name",
    "track_name", "explicit", "popularity", "track_genre"
  ))) %>%
  # Drop loudness per study design: including it would be circular
  # because labels were derived from producer communities associated
  # with loudness practices
  select(-loudness) %>%
  drop_na()

# Remap numeric codes to substantive labels throughout
# so every downstream section sees meaningful names
df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    # Outcome
    production_aesthetic = factor(
      production_aesthetic,
      levels = c("A", "M"),
      labels = c("Alternative", "Maximalist")
    ),
    # Pitch class notation (standard musical keys)
    key = recode(as.character(key),
      "0"  = "C",  "1"  = "C#", "2"  = "D",  "3"  = "D#",
      "4"  = "E",  "5"  = "F",  "6"  = "F#", "7"  = "G",
      "8"  = "G#", "9"  = "A",  "10" = "A#", "11" = "B"
    ),
    # Mode
    mode = recode(as.character(mode),
      "0" = "Minor",
      "1" = "Major"
    ),
    # Time signature
    time_signature = recode(as.character(time_signature),
      "1" = "1/4", "2" = "2/4", "3" = "3/4",
      "4" = "4/4", "5" = "5/4"
    )
  )

cat("After dropping missing values and excluded columns:\n")
cat("Dimensions:", nrow(df), "rows,", ncol(df), "columns\n")
cat("Remaining columns:", paste(names(df), collapse = ", "), "\n")
```

```{r outlier-removal}
# IQR-based outlier removal for duration_ms and tempo only.
# These two features have well-documented extreme values from silence
# tracks and spoken-word recordings that would distort the feature space.
# The remaining continuous features are already bounded in [0,1] by
# Spotify's normalization and require no trimming.

remove_outliers_iqr <- function(data, col) {
  q1  <- quantile(data[[col]], 0.25)
  q3  <- quantile(data[[col]], 0.75)
  iqr <- q3 - q1
  data %>% filter(
    .data[[col]] >= q1 - 1.5 * iqr,
    .data[[col]] <= q3 + 1.5 * iqr
  )
}

n_before <- nrow(df)
df <- remove_outliers_iqr(df, "duration_ms")
cat("Removed", n_before - nrow(df), "rows for duration_ms outliers\n")

n_before <- nrow(df)
df <- remove_outliers_iqr(df, "tempo")
cat("Removed", n_before - nrow(df), "rows for tempo outliers\n")

cat("\nFinal dataset:", nrow(df), "rows\n")
cat("\nFinal class distribution:\n")
print(table(df$production_aesthetic))
cat("\nClass proportions:\n")
print(prop.table(table(df$production_aesthetic)))
```

---

# 5 Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r eda-class-balance, fig.cap="Figure 1. Distribution of production aesthetic labels.", fig.height=8, fig.width=12}
df %>%
  count(production_aesthetic) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = production_aesthetic, y = n,
             fill = production_aesthetic)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, color = "black", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(scales::comma(n), "\n(",
                       scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1), ")")),
    vjust = -0.3, size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "coral")) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::comma,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Production Aesthetic Labels",
    x     = "Production Aesthetic",
    y     = "Number of Songs"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

The dataset is nearly perfectly balanced between the two aesthetic categories. This means accuracy is an interpretable metric, no resampling strategy is required, and the default 0.5 probability threshold for binary classification is well-calibrated with respect to class prevalence.

The dataset is nearly balanced across the two aesthetic categories, with 52,722 alternative songs (48.9 percent) and 55,152 maximalist songs (51.1 percent). The small difference between classes is not substantively meaningful for model estimation and does not indicate systematic class imbalance. As a result, standard evaluation metrics such as accuracy can be interpreted directly without adjustment, and no resampling procedures such as SMOTE or class weighting are required to correct for class imbalance. The default decision threshold of 0.5 is also appropriate given the close alignment between class proportions.

This balance is a structural consequence of the labeling procedure rather than an imposed constraint. The Louvain community detection algorithm partitions the producer co-credit network into two communities that are similar in size under the given initialization, and this partition propagates through producer level assignments to the song level labels. The resulting distribution therefore reflects the organization of the underlying producer network rather than an externally enforced balancing rule.

```{r eda-correlations, fig.cap="Figure 2. Pearson correlation of each audio feature with the production aesthetic label.", fig.height=8, fig.width=12}
df %>%
  mutate(label_numeric = if_else(
    production_aesthetic == "Maximalist", 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  cor() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("feature") %>%
  select(feature, label_numeric) %>%
  filter(feature != "label_numeric") %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(label_numeric))) %>%
  mutate(
    direction = if_else(label_numeric >= 0, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
    feature   = fct_reorder(feature, label_numeric)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = feature, y = label_numeric, fill = direction)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "coral")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Pearson Correlation of Audio Features with Production Aesthetic",
    x     = "Feature",
    y     = "Correlation with Maximalist Label",
    fill  = "Direction"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)
```

The Pearson correlation plot provides an initial overview of which audio features are linearly associated with the aesthetic classification and in which direction. Energy shows the strongest association with the maximalist label, with a correlation of approximately 0.63, indicating that higher energy values are substantially more common among maximalist tracks. This aligns with the characterization of maximalist production as emphasizing perceptual intensity and loudness. Acousticness is the strongest predictor in the alternative direction, with a correlation of approximately negative 0.50, indicating that alternative production is more closely associated with acoustic or less electronically processed recordings. Instrumentalness also shows a notable negative association with maximalism, suggesting that vocal presence is more common in maximalist contexts, while instrumental textures are relatively more characteristic of alternative production.

Tempo and valence exhibit moderate positive correlations with maximalism, consistent with the tendency for mainstream commercial production to favor higher tempo and more positively valenced tracks. Danceability, liveness, speechiness, and duration show weaker associations, indicating that they contribute less linear signal for distinguishing between the two aesthetic categories, even though they may still carry information in combination with other variables.

These results motivate the first of the two hypotheses that are examined in the subsequent modeling section.

H1: A small subset of features, particularly energy and acousticness, is expected to account for most of the predictive signal in distinguishing production aesthetics. 

```{r eda-densities, fig.cap="Figure 3. Distributions of key audio features by production aesthetic.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
df %>%
  select(duration_ms, danceability, energy, speechiness,
         acousticness, instrumentalness, liveness, valence,
         tempo, production_aesthetic) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols     = -production_aesthetic,
    names_to = "feature", values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(feature = str_to_title(
    str_replace_all(feature, "_", " ")
  )) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = production_aesthetic, y = value,
             fill = production_aesthetic)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.8,
               outlier.size = 0.3,
               outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~feature, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "coral")) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Audio Features by Production Aesthetic",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Feature Value",
    fill  = "Production Aesthetic"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text      = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x     = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x    = element_blank()
  )
```

The nine audio features in this dataset are measured on different scales and exhibit substantially different distributional forms. Several variables, including danceability, energy, acousticness, liveness, speechiness, valence, and instrumentalness, are bounded between 0 and 1 as a result of Spotify’s normalization procedure. However, their empirical distributions differ markedly. Some are approximately symmetric, while others are heavily skewed. Instrumentalness in particular is concentrated extremely close to zero across both aesthetic categories, reflecting the fact that most commercially released tracks are vocal recordings. As a result, standard kernel density estimation provides limited additional information for this feature since the distribution is dominated by a near point mass at zero.

Tempo is measured in beats per minute and spans a much wider numerical range, while duration is measured in milliseconds and varies by orders of magnitude relative to the bounded features. Given these differences, a uniform visualization approach must accommodate both bounded and unbounded variables without imposing distributional assumptions that obscure structural differences. Violin plots combined with embedded boxplots are used for this purpose. The violin component summarizes the empirical distribution without relying on parametric density estimation, while the boxplot provides a consistent reference for median and interquartile range. Each feature is displayed on a separate scale to preserve interpretability across heterogeneous measurement units.

The distributional plots provide a more detailed view of aesthetic separation than correlation coefficients alone. The acousticness distribution shows the clearest contrast between categories. Alternative tracks are distributed broadly across the range with substantial mass at higher values, whereas maximalist tracks are heavily concentrated near zero. The energy distribution shows the inverse pattern, with maximalist tracks concentrated toward higher values and alternative tracks more dispersed at lower levels. Together, these patterns support the hypothesis that energy and acousticness are the most diagnostically informative features for distinguishing the two aesthetics, and that even simple threshold rules on either variable would achieve meaningful separation.

Danceability and valence show partial but less distinct separation, with both categories occupying overlapping mid-range distributions and maximalist tracks exhibiting only a modest upward shift. Speechiness and liveness are highly concentrated near-zero for both categories, with differences emerging primarily in the upper tails rather than the bulk of the distributions. Instrumentalness confirms the earlier observation that its predictive contribution is driven by a small number of non vocal recordings that are disproportionately associated with the alternative category.

Tempo exhibits substantial overlap between categories, with only a slight upward shift in the maximalist distribution. Duration shows nearly identical distributions across both aesthetics, indicating minimal discriminatory power in isolation. Overall, the violin plots suggest that while several features operate primarily through shifts in central tendency, others reflect differences in distributional shape or tail behavior, particularly acousticness and instrumentalness.

These patterns provide support for the hypothesis that the aesthetic boundary is not strictly linear in the feature space. While the correlation analysis provides evidence of linear association, it does not capture such multivariate structure, which is assessed in the modeling and distributional analyses that follow. In several cases, separation arises from differences in distributional structure rather than simple mean differences. This suggests that models capable of capturing threshold effects and feature interactions, such as tree-based methods, may better represent the underlying decision boundary than purely linear approaches. This motivates the inclusion of such models in the subsequent analysis and informs the comparison of model performance reported in later sections.

H2: The relationship between audio features and aesthetic category may be non-linear. Tree-based models can perform better than linear models in this case.

---

# 6 Feature Engineering and Data Splitting

```{r feature-engineering}
# One-hot encode key, mode, and time_signature.
# These are nominal categorical variables. Treating them as numeric
# would impose a false ordinal structure (e.g., implying that key G#
# is "between" key G and key A in a meaningful sense).
# Min-max scaling of all numeric predictors to [0,1] ensures that
# tempo and duration_ms, which have large absolute values, do not
# dominate regularization-based or distance-based models purely
# because of their scale.

df_encoded <- df %>%
  mutate(
    key            = factor(key),
    time_signature = factor(time_signature),
    mode           = factor(mode)
  ) %>%
  recipe(production_aesthetic ~ ., data = .) %>%
  step_dummy(key, time_signature, mode, one_hot = TRUE) %>%
  step_range(all_numeric_predictors(), min = 0, max = 1) %>%
  prep() %>%
  bake(new_data = NULL)

cat("Feature matrix after encoding:\n")
cat(nrow(df_encoded), "rows,", ncol(df_encoded) - 1, "features\n")
cat("\nFeature names:\n")
print(names(df_encoded %>% select(-production_aesthetic)))
```

```{r data-split}
# 80/20 stratified train/test split.
# Stratification preserves the near-perfect class balance in both sets.
set.seed(2026)

data_split <- initial_split(df_encoded, prop = 0.8,
                             strata = production_aesthetic)
train_data <- training(data_split)
test_data  <- testing(data_split)

# 5-fold cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning.
# With approximately 91,000 training observations, each fold contains
# roughly 18,000 observations, 5 is sufficient for stable AUC estimates.
# 5-fold is also chosen over 10-fold to reduce computational cost by
# approximately half with negligible impact on estimate stability
# at this sample size.
cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 5, strata = production_aesthetic)

cat("Training set:", nrow(train_data), "rows\n")
cat("Test set    :", nrow(test_data),  "rows\n")
cat("CV folds    :", length(cv_folds$id), "\n")

# Define outcome and feature vectors
outcome  <- "production_aesthetic"
features <- setdiff(names(df_encoded), outcome)

# Numeric 0/1 outcome vectors required by SuperLearner
train_y <- as.numeric(train_data$production_aesthetic == "Maximalist")
test_y  <- as.numeric(test_data$production_aesthetic  == "Maximalist")
train_x <- train_data %>% select(all_of(features))
test_x  <- test_data  %>% select(all_of(features))

cat("\nNumber of features:", length(features), "\n")
cat("Training outcome distribution:\n")
print(table(train_data$production_aesthetic))
cat("Test outcome distribution:\n")
print(table(test_data$production_aesthetic))
```

---

# 7 Model Development and Hyperparameter Tuning

The six models are fit in order of increasing complexity, with each successive model introduced to address limitations of the previous one and to test additional structure in the relationship between audio features and aesthetic labels. This ordering allows the modeling pipeline to move from highly constrained functional forms toward more flexible approaches that can capture interactions and non-linear decision boundaries.

Hyperparameter tuning is conducted within the training set using five-fold cross-validation, with ROC-AUC used as the selection criterion. ROC-AUC is independent of any fixed classification threshold and evaluates the model’s ability to rank positive observations above negative ones across all possible thresholds. This makes it appropriate for tuning because the optimal decision threshold may vary depending on the intended application of the classifier and is not fixed at the modeling stage.

Final performance evaluation on the held-out test set is reserved for Section 8 to ensure that model selection decisions are fully separated from out-of-sample assessment.

## 7.0 Loss Function Helper
```{r loss-function-helper}
# Loss function helper
# Negative log-likelihood (log-loss) for binary classification.
# For a single observation: -(y * log(p) + (1-y) * log(1-p))
# Averaged across n observations:
#   L = -(1/n) * sum[ y_i * log(p_i) + (1 - y_i) * log(1 - p_i) ]
# A small epsilon (1e-15) is added to prevent log(0).
# Lower values indicate better calibrated probability predictions.

log_loss <- function(y_true, y_prob) {
  eps <- 1e-15
  y_prob <- pmax(pmin(y_prob, 1 - eps), eps)
  -mean(y_true * log(y_prob) + (1 - y_true) * log(1 - y_prob))
}

# Gini impurity for a node with class proportions p_1 ... p_K:
#   Gini = 1 - sum(p_k^2)
# For binary classification this simplifies to:
#   Gini = 1 - (p^2 + (1-p)^2) = 2 * p * (1 - p)
# Used as the split criterion in decision trees and random forest.
# Lower values indicate purer nodes (better separation).

gini_impurity <- function(p) {
  2 * p * (1 - p)
}
```

Before fitting any model, two loss functions are defined explicitly as helper functions that are computed and reported consistently across the modeling pipeline. This makes the optimization objective of each method transparent and ensures that performance comparisons are not based on model specific default summaries that may differ across software implementations. It also allows training behavior to be evaluated on a common scale, making it possible to compare how effectively different models reduce the same objective function.

The first helper function computes negative log-likelihood, also referred to as log-loss or binary cross-entropy. For a single observation, the loss equals the negative logarithm of the probability assigned to the true class. If the true label is maximalist, the loss is negative log of the predicted probability of maximalist. If the true label is alternative, the loss is negative log of one minus that probability. Averaging across all observations yields the overall log loss. The measure is bounded below by zero, approaches zero as predictions become both confident and correct, and increases without bound as predictions become confidently incorrect. A small epsilon value of 1e-15 is added inside the logarithm to avoid numerical instability when predicted probabilities reach exactly zero or one. This loss function corresponds to the training objective used in logistic regression, lasso logistic regression, XGBoost with a logistic objective, and the Super Learner meta model, making it a natural measure of optimization fit across these methods.

The second helper function computes Gini impurity, which is the criterion used for splitting in decision trees and random forests. At each candidate split, the algorithm evaluates all possible feature threshold combinations and selects the split that maximizes the reduction in weighted Gini impurity across the resulting nodes. In the binary case, Gini impurity at a node is defined as two times p times one minus p, where p is the proportion of maximalist observations in that node. This quantity reaches its maximum when classes are evenly mixed and equals zero when a node is perfectly pure. The splitting process therefore seeks partitions that progressively reduce impurity by increasing class homogeneity within nodes.

The root node impurity provides a useful baseline for interpreting tree-based models. It reflects the initial heterogeneity of the full dataset before any splits are applied. Comparing this value to the impurity of deeper nodes and to the final predictive performance helps quantify how much structure the tree has extracted from the feature space. A model that achieves strong predictive performance while substantially reducing impurity from the root node has successfully learned meaningful decision boundaries rather than relying on trivial class separation.

## 7.1 Logistic Regression

**Model Introduction:**

Logistic regression estimates a single linear decision boundary in the feature space. It provides a baseline assessment of whether the aesthetic distinction is approximately linearly separable using audio features alone. Its performance establishes a reference point for the rest of the modeling pipeline, since more complex models must demonstrate meaningful improvement over this linear benchmark in order to justify additional flexibility.

The model coefficients are directly interpretable as additive contributions to the log-odds of the maximalist class, holding all other features constant. This allows for an initial assessment of which audio features carry linear signal for distinguishing between the two aesthetic categories and how that signal is distributed across the feature space.

```{r logistic-regression}
lr_spec <- logistic_reg(penalty = 0, mixture = 0) %>%
  set_engine("glm") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

lr_fit <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(lr_spec) %>%
  fit(data = train_data)

# Top coefficients as first read on linear feature signal
lr_coefs <- lr_fit %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(estimate)))

cat("Top 10 logistic regression coefficients (by absolute magnitude):\n")
print(head(lr_coefs, 10))
```

```{r loss-function-lr}
# Loss function: negative log-likelihood
# L = -(1/n) * sum[ y_i * log(p_i) + (1 - y_i) * log(1 - p_i) ]
# Logistic regression minimizes this directly via IRLS during fitting.

lr_train_prob <- lr_fit %>%
  predict(train_data, type = "prob") %>%
  pull(.pred_Maximalist)

lr_train_y <- as.numeric(train_data$production_aesthetic == "Maximalist")

lr_logloss <- log_loss(lr_train_y, lr_train_prob)
cat("Logistic Regression — training log-loss:", round(lr_logloss, 4), "\n")
```

**Hyperparamter Tuning:**

Logistic regression as implemented here has no hyperparameters requiring tuning. The specification sets penalty to zero and mixture to zero, meaning that no regularization is applied and all 28 features after one-hot encoding enter the model with unconstrained coefficients estimated via maximum likelihood using iteratively reweighted least squares. This is a deliberate modeling choice rather than an omission. The purpose of the unregularized model is to establish the linear ceiling, defined as the best achievable performance under a fully flexible linear combination of all available features.

Introducing regularization at this stage would conflate two separate questions, namely whether the aesthetic boundary is approximately linearly separable and which subset of features is strictly necessary for linear prediction. These questions are addressed separately by logistic regression and Lasso respectively, allowing each model to isolate a distinct aspect of the structure in the data.

Because no tuning is required, logistic regression is fit once on the full training set without cross validation. This makes it the fastest model in the pipeline and the most direct benchmark for evaluating whether the additional flexibility introduced by subsequent models yields meaningful improvements over an unrestricted linear baseline.

**Interpretations of the results:**

Logistic regression is the appropriate starting model for this classification problem for two reasons. First, it directly estimates the probability that a song belongs to the maximalist category as a linear function of the audio features, which is the quantity required both for binary classification and for downstream ranking applications that rely on probabilistic scores. Second, the model is fully interpretable, since its coefficients correspond to additive effects on the log odds scale. This makes it possible to obtain an initial, transparent description of how each audio feature contributes to the aesthetic distinction and in which direction, establishing a baseline against which all subsequent models are evaluated.

The loss function minimized during training is the negative log likelihood averaged over all observations. This objective penalizes confident misclassifications more heavily than uncertain ones, meaning that predictions that are both wrong and overconfident incur large losses, while less confident errors are penalized more mildly. As a result, the model is encouraged to produce well calibrated probability estimates rather than only correct binary labels. This is particularly relevant for downstream applications where predicted probabilities may be used for ranking or recommendation rather than hard classification. The training log loss of 0.4085 serves as the baseline performance level. Improvements in more flexible models are meaningful only if they reduce this loss without degrading out of sample performance, while increases in training performance accompanied by poorer test performance indicate overfitting rather than genuine signal extraction.

The coefficient estimates provide direct evidence of the linear structure underlying the aesthetic boundary. Energy has the largest positive coefficient at 9.35, indicating that higher energy values are strongly associated with the maximalist category when holding all other features constant. This aligns with its role as the dominant linear predictor identified in the exploratory analysis. Instrumentalness shows a strong negative coefficient of -2.77, followed by speechiness at -1.61, valence at -1.20, and danceability at 1.18 in the positive direction. Duration and liveness also have negative coefficients, suggesting that longer and more live sounding recordings are more strongly associated with the alternative aesthetic. Tempo has a smaller positive coefficient of 0.298, consistent with its weaker but still positive association with maximalist aesthetic.

Two results are particularly notable. First, acousticness does not appear among the largest coefficients despite its strong marginal correlation with the label in the exploratory analysis. This is consistent with multicollinearity, since acousticness and energy are strongly negatively correlated and the model distributes shared signal primarily through energy when both are included. Second, the indicator for time signature category zero has a large estimated coefficient of 4.05 but a high standard error of 4.64 and a p-value of 3.83e-1, indicating substantial estimation uncertainty due to sparsity. This instability suggests that its apparent effect is not reliable and is expected to be regularized toward zero in the lasso specification.

## 7.2 Lasso Logistic Regression

**Model Introduction:**

Lasso adds L1 regularization, which shrinks uninformative coefficients to exactly zero. With 30-plus features after one-hot encoding, many are likely redundant — particularly the one-hot encoded key and time signature dummies — and Lasso identifies the sparse subset that carries genuine linear signal. It answers the second question: which features are actually necessary for linear classification, and how many can be discarded without losing predictive performance? The penalty parameter is tuned via 5-fold CV over a log-scale grid of 30 values.

```{r lasso}
lasso_spec <- logistic_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 1) %>%
  set_engine("glmnet") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

lasso_grid <- grid_regular(
  penalty(range = c(-4, 0)),
  levels = 30
)

lasso_tune <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(lasso_spec) %>%
  tune_grid(
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid      = lasso_grid,
    metrics   = metric_set(roc_auc)
  )

best_lasso <- select_best(lasso_tune, metric = "roc_auc")
cat("Optimal Lasso penalty:", round(best_lasso$penalty, 6), "\n")

lasso_final <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(lasso_spec) %>%
  finalize_workflow(best_lasso) %>%
  fit(data = train_data)

lasso_coefs <- lasso_final %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(estimate != 0, term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(estimate)))

cat("\nLasso retained", nrow(lasso_coefs), "of", length(features),
    "features\n")
cat("Top 10 retained features:\n")
print(head(lasso_coefs, 10))
```

```{r loss-function-lasso}
# Loss function: L1-penalized negative log-likelihood
# L = -(1/n) * sum[ y_i * log(p_i) + (1 - y_i) * log(1 - p_i) ]
#     + lambda * sum(|beta_j|)
# The penalty term shrinks coefficients toward zero; lambda = 0.0001
# was selected by 5-fold CV to maximize ROC-AUC.
# The CV risk (mean log-loss across folds) at the optimal lambda:

lasso_cv_risk <- lasso_tune %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  filter(penalty == best_lasso$penalty) %>%
  pull(mean)

cat("Lasso — optimal lambda:", round(best_lasso$penalty, 6), "\n")
cat("Lasso — CV mean ROC-AUC at optimal lambda:",
    round(lasso_cv_risk, 4), "\n")

lasso_train_prob <- lasso_final %>%
  predict(train_data, type = "prob") %>%
  pull(.pred_Maximalist)

lasso_logloss <- log_loss(lr_train_y, lasso_train_prob)
cat("Lasso — training log-loss:", round(lasso_logloss, 4), "\n")
```

**Hyperparameter Tuning:**

Lasso logistic regression introduces a single hyperparameter, the penalty strength lambda, which controls how aggressively coefficients are shrunk toward zero during estimation. Unlike standard logistic regression, which estimates all coefficients freely via maximum likelihood, Lasso modifies the objective function by adding a penalty equal to lambda times the sum of the absolute values of the coefficients. As lambda increases, the penalty forces more coefficients to shrink toward exactly zero, producing a sparser model. Selecting an appropriate level of regularization is therefore an empirical question, since the optimal trade-off between sparsity and predictive performance depends on the structure of the feature set and the sample size.

The tuning procedure evaluates 30 values of lambda on a log scale ranging from 1e-4 to 1.0, implemented through a regular grid on the log10 scale. For each candidate value, the model is trained on four folds of the training data and evaluated on the remaining fold using ROC-AUC as the performance metric. This process is repeated across all five folds, and the results are averaged to produce a stable estimate of out-of-sample discrimination at each level of regularization. ROC-AUC is used as the tuning criterion because it is threshold-independent and evaluates the model's ranking performance across all possible classification cutoffs, rather than relying on a fixed decision threshold during model selection. The optimal lambda is chosen as the value that maximizes mean cross-validated ROC-AUC across the grid.

The selected optimal lambda is 1e-4, which lies at the lower boundary of the search space. This indicates that only minimal regularization is required to achieve the best cross-validated performance, suggesting that the feature set does not exhibit severe multicollinearity or overfitting at the current sample size of approximately 86,000 training observations. Consistent with this result, the Lasso retains 27 of 28 features after encoding, removing only a single predictor whose contribution is absorbed by correlated variables. The near-identical performance of Lasso and logistic regression confirms that the linear signal in this dataset is robust and broadly distributed across features rather than concentrated in a small subset of highly predictive variables.

**Interpretations of the results:**

The Lasso results confirm and refine the picture established by logistic regression. The optimal penalty of 1e-04 is selected through cross validation, and the corresponding cross validated ROC-AUC is 0.8923, providing an out of sample estimate of linear classification performance. This value is computed entirely within the training folds and therefore reflects generalization performance under the same sampling distribution without any information leakage from the test set. The training log loss of 0.4086 is nearly identical to that of logistic regression, indicating that the regularization strength is sufficiently small that the fitted solution remains close to the unregularized linear boundary.

The coefficient estimates are highly consistent with logistic regression. Energy remains the dominant predictor at 9.26, essentially unchanged from the unregularized estimate, indicating that its contribution is stable and not driven by multicollinearity artifacts. Instrumentalness, speechiness, valence, danceability, duration, liveness, and tempo all retain their sign and similar magnitudes, suggesting that the linear signal structure is distributed across multiple features rather than concentrated in a small subset. In total, the Lasso retains 26 of 28 features, indicating that very few variables can be removed without some loss of predictive information under a linear specification.

The main differences from logistic regression are limited but informative. First, the slight shrinkage of coefficients under L1 regularization indicates that several features carry overlapping or partially redundant signal, even if they are not fully eliminated. Second, the time_signature_X0 coefficient is reduced from 4.05 to 1.96, reflecting its instability under sparse support and high variance in the unregularized model. The second time signature indicator, time_signature_X1.4, is also reduced in magnitude to -0.224, further suggesting that time signature effects are weak and sensitive to regularization.

Overall, the similarity between logistic regression and Lasso indicates that the linear decision boundary is robust and not dependent on a small subset of predictors. The cross validated ROC-AUC of approximately 0.89 therefore serves as a stable estimate of the linear performance ceiling. Any improvement from more flexible models must come from capturing structure that is not expressible through a sparse or dense linear combination of audio features.

## 7.3 Decision Tree

**Model Introduction:**

A single decision tree sits between the linear models and random forest in the complexity gradient. It partitions the feature space through recursive binary splits, where at each node the algorithm selects the feature and threshold that most reduces Gini impurity in the resulting child nodes. Unlike logistic regression, this structure allows the model to capture nonlinear decision boundaries and threshold effects, such as rules of the form maximalist if energy exceeds a given value. Unlike random forest, a single tree remains fully interpretable, since the complete set of learned split rules can be read directly as a decision path from root to leaf. The model therefore addresses whether a single recursive partitioning of the feature space can recover meaningful aesthetic signal beyond a linear boundary, while also providing a transparent baseline for the ensemble methods that follow.

The cost complexity parameter alpha controls post-pruning of the tree by penalizing additional splits. Higher values of alpha produce simpler trees by removing splits that contribute less to overall fit, thereby reducing variance at the cost of increased bias. The optimal value of alpha is selected using 5-fold cross-validation over a log-scale grid of 30 candidate values, consistent with the tuning strategy used for regularized linear models. This ensures comparability across model classes in how complexity is controlled and selected.

```{r decision-tree}
dt_spec <- decision_tree(
  cost_complexity = tune(),
  tree_depth      = tune(),
  min_n           = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("rpart") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

dt_grid <- grid_regular(
  cost_complexity(range = c(-4, -1)),
  tree_depth(range = c(3, 10)),
  min_n(range = c(5, 30)),
  levels = 3
)

dt_tune <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(dt_spec) %>%
  tune_grid(
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid      = dt_grid,
    metrics   = metric_set(roc_auc)
  )

best_dt <- select_best(dt_tune, metric = "roc_auc")

cat("Best Decision Tree hyperparameters:\n")
cat("  cost_complexity:", round(best_dt$cost_complexity, 6), "\n")
cat("  tree_depth     :", best_dt$tree_depth, "\n")
cat("  min_n          :", best_dt$min_n, "\n")

autoplot(dt_tune) +
  labs(title = "Decision Tree Hyperparameter Tuning Profile") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

dt_final <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(dt_spec) %>%
  finalize_workflow(best_dt) %>%
  fit(data = train_data)

cat("\nFinal decision tree fit complete\n")
cat("  cost_complexity:", round(best_dt$cost_complexity, 6),
    ", tree_depth:", best_dt$tree_depth,
    ", min_n:", best_dt$min_n, "\n")
```

```{r loss-function-dt}
# Loss function: Gini impurity
# At each candidate split, rpart selects the feature and threshold
# that minimizes the weighted average Gini impurity across the two
# child nodes:
#   Gini = 1 - sum(p_k^2)  =  2 * p * (1 - p)  for binary outcomes
# A split is only accepted if it reduces Gini by more than
# cost_complexity * (current tree complexity).
# cost_complexity = 0.001 (tuned value) controls this pruning threshold.

# Observed Gini at the root node (before any split):
root_p <- mean(lr_train_y)   # overall maximalist proportion in training set
root_gini <- gini_impurity(root_p)
cat("Decision Tree — root node Gini impurity:",
    round(root_gini, 4), "\n")
cat("  (= 2 *", round(root_p, 4), "* (1 -",
    round(root_p, 4), ") =", round(root_gini, 4), ")\n")

dt_train_prob <- dt_final %>%
  predict(train_data, type = "prob") %>%
  pull(.pred_Maximalist)

dt_logloss <- log_loss(lr_train_y, dt_train_prob)
cat("Decision Tree — training log-loss:", round(dt_logloss, 4), "\n")
```

**Hyperparameter Tuning:**

The decision tree has three hyperparameters requiring tuning. Cost complexity, also called the pruning parameter alpha, controls how aggressively the fitted tree is pruned after initial growth. Branches whose contribution to reducing impurity is smaller than alpha times the current tree complexity are removed, trading off fit against simplicity. Tree depth sets the maximum number of splits allowed along any path from the root node to a leaf, directly constraining model complexity regardless of pruning. Minimum node size sets the smallest number of training observations required in a node before a split is permitted, preventing the tree from forming very small leaves that overfit local noise.

The tuning grid is constructed using a regular grid with three levels per parameter, producing 27 total combinations. Cost complexity is searched on a log scale from 1e-4 to 0.1, tree depth ranges from 3 to 10, and minimum node size ranges from 5 to 30. The same five fold cross validation procedure used for Lasso is applied, with ROC-AUC as the tuning criterion and select_best used to identify the optimal configuration. Using three levels per parameter, rather than the larger grid used for Lasso, is appropriate because decision tree performance is typically less sensitive to small changes in hyperparameters, and the performance surface over the grid is generally smoother with a broader optimum. This allows a coarser search to identify a stable region of good performance while keeping computation tractable.

**Interpretations of the results:**

The hyperparameter tuning profile reveals a clear and interpretable pattern across all three panels. Cost complexity has the strongest and most consistent effect on cross-validated ROC-AUC. Regardless of tree depth or minimum node size, performance declines sharply as cost complexity increases from 1e-04 toward 1e-01. This indicates that the aesthetic boundary requires a moderately complex tree structure to be effectively captured. Strong pruning consistently removes useful splits and reduces discrimination performance. Tree depth has the second strongest effect. Depth 10 consistently outperforms depth 6, which in turn outperforms depth 3 at low cost complexity values, suggesting that the aesthetic boundary contains structure at multiple levels of the feature space that shallow trees cannot fully represent. The relatively flat performance for depth 3 across cost complexity values reflects a capacity limitation, where the model reaches its expressive ceiling quickly regardless of pruning strength. Minimum node size shows minimal effect across values of 5, 17, and 30, with nearly identical performance profiles across panels. Given the large sample size of approximately 86,000 training observations, the constraint on minimum node size rarely becomes active because most candidate splits already involve sufficiently large nodes.

The optimal combination selected by cross-validated ROC-AUC is cost complexity of 1e-04, tree depth of 10, and minimum node size of 30. The selection of both minimal cost complexity and maximal tree depth confirms the interpretation of the tuning profile. The cross-validation procedure finds no evidence that pruning or depth restriction improves generalization, and instead favors the most expressive tree permitted by the grid. This suggests that a single decision tree, when allowed sufficient depth and minimal pruning, approaches the performance level of the linear models while relying on recursive partitioning rather than a linear combination of features.

The root node Gini impurity of 0.4997 is effectively the theoretical maximum for a binary classification setting, which occurs when class proportions are nearly equal. Substituting the observed proportions of 0.5113 and 0.4887 yields 2 times 0.5113 times 0.4887 equals 0.4997. This reflects the near-balanced class distribution established in the data preparation stage. The tree therefore begins from near maximal uncertainty and must reduce impurity substantially through successive splits in order to produce meaningful predictions.

The training log-loss of 0.3685 is lower than that of both logistic regression at 0.4085 and Lasso at 0.4086. This indicates that the decision tree fits the training data more closely than the linear models. Whether this improvement reflects genuine nonlinear structure or early signs of overfitting is not determined at the training stage. This distinction is evaluated in the held-out test set results in Section 7, where a substantial gap between training and test log-loss would indicate limited generalization.

## 7.4 Random Forest

**Model Introduction:**

Random forest grows many decorrelated decision trees by bootstrapping the training data and restricting each split to a random subset of features. Predictions are obtained by averaging across all trees, which reduces variance while maintaining low bias. Unlike linear models, random forest can capture nonlinear relationships and feature interactions, such as cases where the effect of energy depends on the level of acousticness. It therefore addresses the question of how much additional predictive signal exists beyond a linear decision boundary.

The hyperparameters tuned are mtry, which controls the number of features considered at each split, and min_n, which sets the minimum number of observations required in a node before further splitting is allowed. Together, these parameters regulate the trade-off between tree diversity, depth, and overfitting. During tuning, the number of trees is fixed at 100 to reduce computation time while preserving stable estimates of performance, and the final model is refit using 500 trees to ensure convergence of the ensemble prediction.

```{r random-forest}
cl <- makePSOCKcluster(max(1, detectCores() - 1))
registerDoParallel(cl)

rf_spec_tune <- rand_forest(
  mtry  = tune(),
  trees = 100,
  min_n = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("ranger") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

rf_grid <- grid_regular(
  mtry(range  = c(3, 15)),
  min_n(range = c(5, 30)),
  levels = 3
)

rf_tune <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(rf_spec_tune) %>%
  tune_grid(
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid      = rf_grid,
    metrics   = metric_set(roc_auc)
  )

stopCluster(cl)

best_rf <- select_best(rf_tune, metric = "roc_auc")
cat("Best RF hyperparameters:\n")
cat("  mtry  :", best_rf$mtry,  "\n")
cat("  min_n :", best_rf$min_n, "\n")

autoplot(rf_tune) +
  labs(title = "Random Forest Hyperparameter Tuning Profile") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

# Refit with 500 trees and permutation importance for Section 10
rf_final <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(
    rand_forest(
      mtry  = best_rf$mtry,
      min_n = best_rf$min_n,
      trees = 500
    ) %>%
      set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") %>%
      set_mode("classification")
  ) %>%
  fit(data = train_data)

cat("\nFinal random forest fit: 500 trees,",
    "mtry =", best_rf$mtry,
    ", min_n =", best_rf$min_n, "\n")
```

```{r loss-function-rf}
# Loss function: Gini impurity (same formula as decision tree)
# Each of the 500 trees selects splits by minimizing weighted Gini
# across child nodes. Because only mtry = 9 randomly selected features
# are considered at each split, trees are decorrelated — each sees a
# different random subset of features, so their errors are independent
# and average out when predictions are combined.

rf_train_prob <- predict(rf_final, train_data, type = "prob") %>%
  pull(.pred_Maximalist)

rf_logloss <- log_loss(lr_train_y, rf_train_prob)
cat("Random Forest — training log-loss:", round(rf_logloss, 4), "\n")
```

**Hyperparameter Tuning:**

Random forest has two hyperparameters tuned in this analysis, while the number of trees is held fixed. The first is mtry, which determines how many features are randomly sampled as candidates at each split. Lower values of mtry increase diversity across trees because each tree is forced to consider different subsets of predictors, which reduces correlation between trees and improves variance reduction when predictions are averaged. Higher values allow each split to access more information, but increase correlation across trees, which weakens the benefit of ensembling. A common heuristic for classification problems is the square root of the number of features, which is approximately 5 in this setting with 28 predictors, although the optimal value is ultimately data dependent.

The second hyperparameter is minimum node size, which controls how many observations must be present in a node before it can be split further. Smaller values allow deeper trees that capture more localized structure in the feature space, while larger values constrain depth and reduce variance at the cost of bias.

The tuning grid evaluates mtry from 3 to 15 and minimum node size from 5 to 30, using three levels per parameter for a total of nine combinations. Each model in the grid is trained using 100 trees rather than the final configuration of 500 trees. This reduces computational cost substantially while maintaining stable estimates of cross validated performance, since random forest performance typically stabilizes well before the maximum number of trees is reached in datasets of this size. Computation is further accelerated through parallel processing using a PSOCK cluster setup with all available cores.

The optimal configuration selects mtry equal to 9 and minimum node size equal to 5. The selected mtry is notably higher than the square root heuristic, suggesting that a larger subset of candidate features per split is beneficial in this setting. This is consistent with earlier findings from Lasso and logistic regression, which indicate that predictive signal is distributed across many features rather than concentrated in a small subset. In such a setting, allowing more candidate variables at each split increases the likelihood that informative predictors are considered at each decision point, improving overall ensemble performance. After tuning, 500 trees are used instead.

**Interpretations of the results:**

The hyperparameter tuning profile for random forest reveals a clear and consistent pattern across both hyperparameters. Minimum node size of 5 produces the highest cross-validated ROC-AUC at every level of mtry, with minimum node size 17 performing second and minimum node size 30 performing third. This ordering is stable across the full mtry range and indicates that allowing trees to grow to finer granularity, with terminal nodes containing as few as 5 observations, improves discrimination performance. Given a training set size of approximately 86,000 observations, nodes of this size represent a very small fraction of the data, suggesting that the aesthetic boundary contains local structure that benefits from deep partitioning rather than coarse aggregation.

The effect of mtry is similarly systematic. Performance increases as mtry rises from 3 to 9 across all levels of minimum node size, then plateaus between 9 and 15. This pattern indicates that considering more candidate features per split improves the likelihood of selecting informative predictors up to a point, after which additional candidates provide diminishing returns. The plateau beyond mtry of 9 suggests that this level is sufficient to reliably include the most predictive features at each split, and that increasing it further mainly increases computational cost without improving discrimination. The selected optimal configuration of mtry 9 and minimum node size 5 lies at the peak of this performance surface, with cross-validated ROC-AUC around 0.955.

This performance represents a substantial improvement over the linear ceiling of approximately 0.893 established by logistic regression and Lasso. The gap in ROC-AUC provides direct evidence that the aesthetic boundary is not linearly separable in the audio feature space and instead contains nonlinear interactions and threshold effects that are captured by the ensemble of decorrelated trees. Random forest recovers this additional signal through repeated partitioning of the feature space across many bootstrap samples.

The training log-loss of 0.0921 is substantially lower than that of the linear models, which is expected for an ensemble of deep decision trees. Each tree fits its bootstrap sample closely, and averaging across 500 trees yields highly confident predictions on the training data. The key diagnostic is not the absolute value of training loss but the relationship between training and test performance, which determines whether this improvement reflects genuine generalization or overfitting. This is evaluated in the held-out test set results in later sections. The final model is refit using 500 trees at the optimal hyperparameter configuration with permutation-based importance enabled to support downstream interpretability in the feature importance analysis.

## 7.5 XGBoost

**Model Introduction:**

Gradient boosting builds trees sequentially, with each new tree trained to correct the residual errors of the current ensemble. This structure makes it particularly effective in regions where the two aesthetic classes overlap, as seen in the density plots, since the algorithm progressively allocates more capacity to observations that earlier trees consistently misclassify. It therefore addresses the question of whether iterative residual fitting can recover additional signal beyond what a single-pass ensemble such as random forest can capture.

Four hyperparameters are tuned in this model: number of trees, tree depth, learning rate, and minimum node size. The number of trees controls how many boosting iterations are performed, with each iteration adding a new corrective tree. Tree depth determines the complexity of interactions each individual tree can represent. Learning rate scales the contribution of each tree, controlling how aggressively the model updates at each step and improving generalization by preventing any single tree from dominating the ensemble. Minimum node size constrains splitting by requiring a minimum number of observations in each node, reducing the risk of overfitting to small local patterns.

The tuning grid evaluates three levels per parameter, resulting in 81 total combinations. This search is computationally feasible at this sample size when combined with parallel processing, and it allows systematic exploration of the trade-off between model complexity and generalization performance.

```{r xgboost}
cl <- makePSOCKcluster(max(1, detectCores() - 1))
registerDoParallel(cl)

xgb_spec <- boost_tree(
  trees      = tune(),
  tree_depth = tune(),
  learn_rate = tune(),
  min_n      = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("xgboost") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

xgb_grid <- grid_regular(
  trees(range      = c(100, 500)),
  tree_depth(range = c(3, 8)),
  learn_rate(range = c(-3, -1)),
  min_n(range      = c(5, 30)),
  levels = 3
)

xgb_tune <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(xgb_spec) %>%
  tune_grid(
    resamples = cv_folds,
    grid      = xgb_grid,
    metrics   = metric_set(roc_auc)
  )

stopCluster(cl)

best_xgb <- select_best(xgb_tune, metric = "roc_auc")
cat("Best XGBoost hyperparameters:\n")
print(best_xgb %>% select(-`.config`))

autoplot(xgb_tune) +
  labs(title = "XGBoost Hyperparameter Tuning Profile") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

xgb_final <- workflow() %>%
  add_formula(production_aesthetic ~ .) %>%
  add_model(xgb_spec) %>%
  finalize_workflow(best_xgb) %>%
  fit(data = train_data)

cat("\nXGBoost final model fit complete\n")
```

```{r loss-function-xgboost}
# Loss function: binary cross-entropy (log-loss), same formula as
# logistic regression but minimized sequentially:
#   L = -(1/n) * sum[ y_i * log(p_i) + (1 - y_i) * log(1 - p_i) ]
# Each boosting round fits a new tree to the negative gradient of this
# loss with respect to the current ensemble's predictions, then adds
# it to the ensemble scaled by learn_rate = 0.1.
# Objective in xgboost: "binary:logistic"

xgb_train_prob <- xgb_final %>%
  predict(train_data, type = "prob") %>%
  pull(.pred_Maximalist)

xgb_logloss <- log_loss(lr_train_y, xgb_train_prob)
cat("XGBoost — training log-loss:", round(xgb_logloss, 4), "\n")
```

**Hyperparameter Tuning:**

XGBoost has four hyperparameters tuned in this analysis, making it the most flexible and most complex model in the pipeline. The number of trees controls how many boosting rounds are performed, with each round adding a new tree that learns to correct the residual errors of the current ensemble. Tree depth determines the maximum depth of each individual tree and therefore the complexity of feature interactions that can be represented within a single tree. Depth one corresponds to additive main effects, depth two allows pairwise interactions, and increasing depth permits progressively higher order interaction structure. Learning rate, or shrinkage, scales the contribution of each tree before it is added to the ensemble, ensuring that the model updates gradually and reducing the risk that any single tree dominates the learning process. Minimum node size controls the smallest number of observations required for a split, preventing the model from fitting extremely small and potentially noisy subgroups.

The tuning grid evaluates three levels per parameter, producing 81 total combinations. Trees are evaluated over a range from 100 to 500, tree depth ranges from 3 to 8, learning rate is searched on a log scale from 0.001 to 0.1, and minimum node size ranges from 5 to 30. As with the random forest, computation is accelerated through parallel processing. The optimal configuration selected by five-fold cross-validated ROC-AUC uses 500 trees, a tree depth of 8, a learning rate of 0.1, and a minimum node size of 5.

The selected tree depth of 8 is relatively high compared to common defaults in gradient boosting, where shallower trees are often preferred. This suggests that the relationship between audio features and aesthetic labels involves higher-order interactions that benefit from deeper hierarchical partitioning within individual trees. The learning rate of 0.1, combined with the maximum number of trees in the grid, indicates that stronger updates across boosting rounds improve performance in this setting, consistent with a feature space that contains substantial predictive signal that can be captured effectively without requiring extremely slow incremental learning.

**Interpretations of the results:**

The XGBoost tuning profile is a nine-panel grid that simultaneously displays the effects of all four hyperparameters, making it the richest diagnostic view in the pipeline. Reading across the panels reveals three consistent patterns.

First, learning rate has the largest effect on cross-validated ROC-AUC. Within every combination of tree depth and number of trees, the rightmost column with learning rate 0.1 consistently outperforms the middle column at 0.01, which in turn outperforms the leftmost column at 0.001. This indicates that the boosting process benefits from relatively large updates at each iteration rather than highly conservative incremental adjustments. The stability of this pattern across all other hyperparameter settings suggests that the signal is strong enough that faster convergence improves discrimination without inducing instability within the explored tree range.

Second, tree depth has the second largest effect. The bottom row at depth 8 consistently achieves higher ROC-AUC than depth 5, which in turn outperforms depth 3. This mirrors the decision tree results, where maximum depth was also selected, and reinforces the interpretation that the aesthetic boundary involves higher-order feature interactions. Increasing depth expands the model’s ability to represent complex interaction structures, since deeper trees can encode multi-feature splits that shallow trees cannot express. The tuning profile indicates that this additional representational capacity translates into measurable gains in classification performance.

Third, the number of trees has a consistent but smaller effect. Increasing the number of trees generally improves ROC-AUC across all panels, with diminishing returns as the number approaches 500. This is consistent with gradient boosting dynamics, where successive trees correct residual errors but contribute progressively smaller improvements as the ensemble converges. Minimum node size shows minimal variation across all panels, with nearly overlapping performance curves for values 5, 17, and 30. This aligns with earlier findings from random forest, where this constraint rarely becomes binding at the current sample size.

The optimal configuration of 500 trees, tree depth 8, learning rate 0.1, and minimum node size 5 lies in the highest-performing region of the grid, located in the bottom right portion of the tuning surface. The training log-loss of 0.163 sits between the linear models at approximately 0.408 and random forest at 0.092. This ordering reflects the structural position of XGBoost in the complexity hierarchy, where sequential residual fitting allows more flexibility than linear models but typically produces less extreme training fit than fully grown bagged trees. Whether this intermediate training fit translates into superior generalization relative to the other models is evaluated using the held-out test set in Section 7.

## 7.6 Super Learner

**Model Introduction:**

The Super Learner is a stacked ensemble that uses cross-validated out-of-fold predictions from all candidate learners as inputs to a non-negative least-squares meta-learner. It is guaranteed to perform at least as well as its best individual component in terms of cross-validated risk. Beyond performance, its learner weights answer a direct substantive question about how much of the aesthetic boundary is captured by linear versus non-linear models. A high aggregate weight on tree-based learners indicates that the aesthetic signal involves feature interactions that cannot be captured by a purely linear boundary.

The candidate library is chosen for both diversity and computational tractability at 114,000 observations. SL.glm and three variants of SL.glmnet cover the linear signal at different regularization strengths. SL.ranger and SL.xgboost cover non-linear and interactive signal. SL.gam captures smooth non-linearities without full tree-based complexity. SL.lda provides a fast parametric geometric classifier. SL.mean is an intercept-only baseline, and a well-functioning ensemble should assign it near-zero weight. Computationally expensive learners including SL.randomForest, SL.nnet, SL.qda, SL.bayesglm, and SL.step.interaction are excluded as at this sample size they would make 5-fold cross-validation impractical without meaningful performance gains over SL.ranger and SL.xgboost.

```{r super-learner}
train_x_mat <- as.data.frame(train_x)
test_x_mat  <- as.data.frame(test_x)

# Custom elastic net wrappers with explicit alpha values
SL.glmnet.0.25 <- function(..., alpha = 0.25) SL.glmnet(..., alpha = alpha)
SL.glmnet.0.75 <- function(..., alpha = 0.75) SL.glmnet(..., alpha = alpha)

sl_library <- c(
  "SL.glm",         # Logistic regression — linear baseline
```
