```{r super-learner}
  "SL.glmnet",      # Elastic net (alpha = 0.5)
  "SL.glmnet.0.25", # Ridge-leaning elastic net
  "SL.glmnet.0.75", # Lasso-leaning elastic net
  "SL.ranger",      # Fast parallel random forest
  "SL.xgboost",     # Gradient boosting
  "SL.gam",         # Smooth non-linearities
  "SL.lda",         # Linear discriminant analysis
  "SL.mean"         # Intercept-only baseline
)

num_cores <- max(1, detectCores() - 1)
cl <- makeCluster(num_cores)
clusterEvalQ(cl, {
  library(SuperLearner)
  library(glmnet)
  library(ranger)
  library(xgboost)
  library(gam)
  library(MASS)
})
clusterExport(cl, c("SL.glmnet.0.25", "SL.glmnet.0.75"))

cat("Fitting Super Learner across", num_cores, "cores with",
    length(sl_library), "candidate learners...\n")

sl_fit <- snowSuperLearner(
  Y          = train_y,
  X          = train_x_mat,
  family     = binomial(),
  SL.library = sl_library,
  cvControl  = list(V = 5),
  cluster    = cl
)

stopCluster(cl)

cat("\nSuper Learner learner weights:\n")
sl_weights <- data.frame(
  learner = names(sl_fit$coef),
  weight  = round(sl_fit$coef, 4)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(weight)) %>%
  mutate(
    model_type = case_when(
      str_detect(learner, "glm|lda")    ~ "Linear",
      str_detect(learner, "ranger|xgb") ~ "Non-linear (Tree)",
      str_detect(learner, "gam")        ~ "Semi-parametric",
      str_detect(learner, "mean")       ~ "Baseline",
      TRUE                              ~ "Other"
    )
  )

sl_weights %>%
  mutate(out = paste0(
    "  ", str_pad(learner, 25, "right"),
    ": ", str_pad(weight, 7, "left"),
    "  (", model_type, ")"
  )) %>%
  pull(out) %>%
  cat(sep = "\n")

cat("\nAggregated weight by model family:\n")
sl_weights %>%
  group_by(model_type) %>%
  summarise(total_weight = round(sum(weight), 4), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(total_weight)) %>%
  mutate(out = paste0(
    "  ", str_pad(model_type, 25, "right"), ": ", total_weight
  )) %>%
  pull(out) %>%
  cat(sep = "\n")
```

```{r loss-function-sl}
# Loss function: cross-validated negative log-likelihood (log-loss)
# The Super Learner meta-learner minimizes:
#   L = -(1/n) * sum[ y_i * log(p_i) + (1 - y_i) * log(1 - p_i) ]
# using out-of-fold predictions from each candidate learner as inputs.
# sl_fit$cvRisk reports the cross-validated log-loss for each candidate.

cat("\nSuper Learner — cross-validated risk (log-loss) per candidate:\n")
cv_risk_df <- data.frame(
  learner  = names(sl_fit$cvRisk),
  cv_risk  = round(sl_fit$cvRisk, 4)
) %>%
  arrange(cv_risk)

print(cv_risk_df)

sl_train_prob <- as.numeric(
  predict(sl_fit, train_x_mat, onlySL = TRUE)$pred
)
sl_logloss <- log_loss(lr_train_y, sl_train_prob)
cat("\nSuper Learner — training log-loss:", round(sl_logloss, 4), "\n")
```

**Hyperparameter Tuning:**

The Super Learner does not tune hyperparameters in the conventional sense. Instead of searching over a grid of values within a single model class, it combines multiple model classes using cross-validated out-of-fold predictions from each candidate learner as inputs to a meta-learner. In this implementation, the meta-learner is a non-negative least squares optimization that learns weights over candidate models by minimizing cross-validated log-loss on the training set. This shifts the notion of tuning from within-model calibration to across-model aggregation, where the primary object of optimization is the convex combination of learners rather than the parameters of any single learner.

The candidate library is designed to span both linear and non-linear regions of the modeling space. SL.glm and multiple SL.glmnet specifications with different elastic-net mixing parameters capture linear structure under varying degrees of regularization. SL.gam introduces smooth non-linear effects without relying on tree-based partitioning. SL.ranger and SL.xgboost capture non-linear interactions and threshold effects through ensemble tree methods. SL.lda provides a parametric linear discriminant baseline, while SL.mean serves as an intercept-only reference model. The weight assigned to SL.mean functions as a diagnostic check, since a near-zero value indicates that the ensemble is extracting meaningful structure beyond a naive prevalence-based prediction.

The resulting weights provide a direct answer at the level of model families rather than individual parameters. High weight concentrated on linear learners would indicate that the aesthetic boundary is largely additive in the audio feature space, while dominance of tree-based learners would suggest that non-linear interactions and threshold effects are essential. In this way, the Super Learner aggregates evidence from the full model spectrum and reveals which functional form best explains the structure of the classification problem.

**Interpretations of the results:**

The cross-validated risk table reports the mean log-loss for each candidate learner evaluated on held-out fold predictions. This provides the quantitative foundation for the weight assignments. Cross-validated risk is a more reliable performance estimate within the training set because each learner is evaluated only on observations that were not used during fitting, which avoids the optimistic bias that appears in training loss.

SL.ranger achieves the lowest cross-validated log-loss at 0.0833, which explains why it receives a weight of 1.0 in the Super Learner. The difference between SL.ranger at 0.0833 and the next best model, SL.xgboost at 0.1041, is 0.0208 log-loss units. This is a meaningful gap, indicating that SL.ranger assigns consistently higher probability to the correct class across held-out folds. The meta-learner weight assignment therefore reflects a clear separation in performance rather than a marginal difference that could easily reverse.

The ranking of the remaining learners is also informative. SL.gam at 0.1250 performs better than all linear and regularized linear models, which cluster tightly between 0.1308 and 0.1309. This suggests that the aesthetic boundary contains smooth non-linear patterns that GAM can capture but linear models cannot. The near-identical performance of SL.glm, SL.glmnet, SL.glmnet.0.25, and SL.glmnet.0.75 confirms earlier findings that the linear signal is stable across different regularization strengths, with no meaningful change in predictive performance across penalties. SL.lda at 0.1346 performs slightly worse than the logistic family, consistent with stronger distributional assumptions that are less well matched to the data. SL.mean at 0.2499 serves as the baseline, corresponding to a model that ignores all features and predicts the majority class probability. All other learners substantially outperform this baseline, confirming that the feature set contains strong predictive signal.

The Super Learner training log-loss of 0.0844 is slightly higher than SL.ranger cross-validated risk at 0.0833. This difference is expected because training log-loss is computed on the full training set using the fitted ensemble, while cross-validated risk is computed from held-out fold predictions during stacking. The close agreement between these values indicates that the ensemble is stable and not overfitting within the training procedure, and suggests that performance on the held-out test set in Section 7 should be similar to the cross-validated estimates.

## 7.7 Loss Function Summary

```{r loss-function-summary}
# Loss function summary across all six models
loss_summary <- tibble(
  Model = c("Logistic Regression", "Lasso", "Decision Tree",
            "Random Forest", "XGBoost", "Super Learner"),
  `Loss Function` = c(
    "Negative log-likelihood",
    "L1-penalized neg. log-likelihood",
    "Gini impurity",
    "Gini impurity (avg. across 500 trees)",
    "Binary cross-entropy (sequential)",
    "Cross-validated neg. log-likelihood"
  ),
  `Training Log-Loss` = c(
    round(lr_logloss,    4),
    round(lasso_logloss, 4),
    round(dt_logloss,    4),
    round(rf_logloss,    4),
    round(xgb_logloss,   4),
    round(sl_logloss,    4)
  )
)

cat("Loss function summary:\n")
print(loss_summary)
```

The loss function summary table brings together training log-loss values computed consistently across all six models using the same helper function. This enables direct comparison of how well each model fits the training data under its own optimization target. Two patterns stand out.

The first pattern is the clear separation between linear models and tree-based models. Logistic regression and Lasso both achieve a training log-loss of 0.408, which represents the floor of what any linear combination of the 28 audio features can achieve on this training set. The decision tree at 0.365 is the first model to meaningfully break below this floor. It shows that recursive binary partitioning captures structure in the training data that no additive linear model can represent. Random forest at 0.092 and the Super Learner at 0.084 extend this improvement substantially. This reflects the ability of deep ensemble methods to fit complex patterns across the feature space. XGBoost at 0.163 sits between the decision tree and random forest. This is consistent with its position in the complexity gradient as a sequential ensemble that corrects residuals step by step rather than averaging fully grown trees.

The second pattern concerns how training log-loss relates to each model's own optimization target. Logistic regression, Lasso, XGBoost, and the Super Learner directly minimize log-loss during training, so their training log-loss values reflect how well each model achieved its stated objective. The decision tree and random forest instead optimize Gini impurity during training, but their log-loss is reported here afterward using the same metric for comparison. The decision tree's training log-loss of 0.365 is therefore not the quantity it directly optimized but a post hoc measure of probability quality on the training data. The same applies to random forest at 0.092. That random forest achieves lower training log-loss than XGBoost despite optimizing a different criterion reflects its use of deep, decorrelated trees that produce very confident predictions on the training set rather than a more directly calibrated optimization objective.

One important limitation applies to all figures in this table. Training log-loss is an optimistic measure of performance because every model has already seen the training data during fitting and can adapt to its specific patterns. A model with very low training log-loss is not necessarily a better generalizer than one with higher values. It may simply be capturing noise that does not hold in new data. The test set evaluation in later sections provides the unbiased assessment of generalization, and the gap between training and test performance for each model serves as a diagnostic of overfitting.

## 7.8 Hyperparameter Tuning Summary

```{r hyperparameter-tuning-summary}
# Hyperparameter summary across all tuned models
hp_summary <- tibble(
  Model = c(
    "Lasso",
    "Decision Tree", "Decision Tree", "Decision Tree",
    "Random Forest", "Random Forest",
    "XGBoost", "XGBoost", "XGBoost", "XGBoost"
  ),
  Hyperparameter = c(
    "penalty (lambda)",
    "cost_complexity", "tree_depth", "min_n",
    "mtry", "min_n",
    "trees", "tree_depth", "learn_rate", "min_n"
  ),
  Definition = c(
    "L1 regularization strength; larger = more coefficients zeroed",
    "Pruning penalty; larger = simpler tree",
    "Max splits root to leaf; caps complexity",
    "Min observations to split a node; prevents tiny leaves",
    "Features considered per split; controls tree decorrelation",
    "Min observations to split a node",
    "Number of boosting rounds",
    "Max depth per tree; controls interaction order",
    "Shrinkage per tree; smaller = more conservative",
    "Min observations to split a node"
  ),
  `Optimal Value` = c(
    round(best_lasso$penalty, 6),
    round(best_dt$cost_complexity, 6),
    best_dt$tree_depth,
    best_dt$min_n,
    best_rf$mtry,
    best_rf$min_n,
    best_xgb$trees,
    best_xgb$tree_depth,
    best_xgb$learn_rate,
    best_xgb$min_n
  )
)

cat("Hyperparameter summary:\n")
print(hp_summary)
```

The hyperparameter summary table consolidates tuning decisions across all models that required cross-validated selection, and reading across rows reveals coherent patterns that connect the individual model results into a unified picture of what the aesthetic classification problem requires.

The Lasso penalty of 0.0001 at the lower bound of the search grid confirms that the linear signal in this feature set does not require strong regularization to generalize. With approximately 86,000 training observations and 28 features, the dataset is large relative to the feature space, so overfitting is not a major concern for linear models. The optimal strategy is therefore to retain nearly all available features with minimal shrinkage rather than enforcing sparsity.

For the decision tree, the combination of minimum cost complexity at 0.0001 and maximum tree depth at 10 indicates that the cross-validation procedure found no benefit from pruning or depth restriction, and the best-performing tree is the deepest and least constrained option in the search grid. The minimum node size of 30 is the only apparent exception, but as discussed in Section 6.3 it had no meaningful effect on cross-validated performance across its range, so its selected value should not be interpreted as evidence of a true preference for larger nodes.

For random forest, the selected mtry of 9 is notably above the conventional square root heuristic of approximately 5 for 28 features. This suggests that many features carry useful signal: when informative variables are abundant, evaluating more candidates per split improves split quality without eliminating the benefit of decorrelation, since even with 9 candidates there remains substantial randomness across trees. The minimum node size of 5, the smallest value in the grid, aligns with earlier findings that deeper trees with smaller leaf nodes consistently improve performance.

For XGBoost, all four selected values sit at or near the most complex end of their respective search ranges, including 500 trees, tree depth 8, learning rate 0.1, and minimum node size 5. This consistent selection of high complexity across all dimensions suggests that the model continues to benefit from added capacity throughout the grid and that the aesthetic boundary is complex enough that underfitting is a more relevant concern than overfitting within the tested range. It also leaves open the possibility that extending the grid further could yield additional gains, particularly for tree depth and number of trees.

Taken together, the hyperparameter table shows a consistent pattern across model families in which added flexibility is repeatedly favored over constraint. No model benefits from heavy regularization, shallow trees, or aggressive pruning. This convergence across independently tuned models supports the interpretation that the aesthetic boundary in the audio feature space is structurally complex, involving non-linear relationships and feature interactions that simpler model forms cannot capture adequately.

---

# 8 Model Evaluation

## 8.1 Evaluation Helper and Predictions

A single helper function generates predictions and computes the full metric suite consistently across all five models. Running evaluation here rather than inside each model section keeps the development and assessment phases cleanly separated.

```{r eval-helper}
# evaluate_model():
#   preds_tbl  — tibble with columns: truth (factor), .pred_prob (numeric)
#   model_name — character label
# Returns a one-row tibble of all metrics

evaluate_model <- function(preds_tbl, model_name) {
  preds_tbl <- preds_tbl %>%
    mutate(
      .pred_class = factor(
        if_else(.pred_prob >= 0.5, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
        levels = levels(truth)
      )
    )

  hard <- metric_set(accuracy, precision, recall, f_meas)(
    preds_tbl,
    truth       = truth,
    estimate    = .pred_class,
    event_level = "second"
  )

  soft <- roc_auc(
    preds_tbl,
    truth       = truth,
    .pred_prob,
    event_level = "second"
  )

  bind_rows(hard, soft) %>%
    select(.metric, .estimate) %>%
    mutate(Model = model_name)
}
```

```{r generate-predictions}
# Generate prediction tibbles for all six models
lr_preds <- lr_fit %>%
  predict(test_data, type = "prob") %>%
  transmute(
    truth      = test_data$production_aesthetic,
    .pred_prob = .pred_Maximalist
  )

lasso_preds <- lasso_final %>%
  predict(test_data, type = "prob") %>%
  transmute(
    truth      = test_data$production_aesthetic,
    .pred_prob = .pred_Maximalist
  )

dt_preds <- dt_final %>%
  predict(test_data, type = "prob") %>%
  transmute(
    truth      = test_data$production_aesthetic,
    .pred_prob = .pred_Maximalist
  )

rf_preds <- predict(rf_final, test_data, type = "prob") %>%
  transmute(
    truth      = test_data$production_aesthetic,
    .pred_prob = .pred_Maximalist
  )

xgb_preds <- xgb_final %>%
  predict(test_data, type = "prob") %>%
  transmute(
    truth      = test_data$production_aesthetic,
    .pred_prob = .pred_Maximalist
  )

sl_preds_prob <- as.numeric(
  predict(sl_fit, test_x_mat, onlySL = TRUE)$pred
)
sl_preds <- tibble(
  truth      = factor(
    ifelse(test_y == 1, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
    levels = c("Alternative", "Maximalist")
  ),
  .pred_prob = sl_preds_prob
)

# Run evaluation helper for all six models
lr_metrics    <- evaluate_model(lr_preds,    "Logistic Regression")
lasso_metrics <- evaluate_model(lasso_preds, "Lasso")
dt_metrics    <- evaluate_model(dt_preds, "Decision Tree")
rf_metrics    <- evaluate_model(rf_preds,    "Random Forest")
xgb_metrics   <- evaluate_model(xgb_preds,  "XGBoost")
sl_metrics    <- evaluate_model(sl_preds,    "Super Learner")

# Combine into single summary table
all_metrics <- bind_rows(
  lr_metrics, lasso_metrics, dt_metrics,
  rf_metrics, xgb_metrics, sl_metrics
) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = .estimate) %>%
  arrange(desc(roc_auc))

cat("Performance summary (ranked by ROC-AUC):\n")
all_metrics %>% 
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4))) %>% 
  print(n = Inf)
```

The performance summary table show several patterns.

The first and most important pattern is the performance gap between linear models and tree-based models. Logistic regression and Lasso both achieve a ROC-AUC of 0.890, an accuracy of 0.809, and an F1-score of 0.818, and are virtually identical across all metrics. This confirms that both models learn the same linear boundary and that L1 regularization at the optimal penalty adds no meaningful improvement beyond unregularized logistic regression. The linear ceiling for this classification problem is therefore established at a ROC-AUC of 0.890. Any model that does not meaningfully exceed this level would not justify added complexity over a simple logistic regression.

The decision tree at ROC-AUC 0.902 is the first model to move above the linear ceiling, exceeding it by about 1.2 percentage points. This shows that the aesthetic boundary contains non-linear structure that a single recursive partition of the feature space can capture, even though the improvement is modest compared to ensemble methods. The decision tree's accuracy of 0.831 and F1-score of 0.839 similarly sit above the linear models but well below random forest and XGBoost, placing it as a transitional model in the complexity gradient that demonstrates non-linearity without fully exploiting it.

XGBoost at ROC-AUC 0.945 represents a substantial jump, exceeding the linear ceiling by 5.5 percentage points and the decision tree by 4.3 points. This gap quantifies the additional signal recovered by sequential residual fitting beyond what a single tree can learn. Random forest at ROC-AUC 0.959 further improves performance and slightly exceeds XGBoost by 1.4 percentage points, achieving the highest accuracy at 0.894 and the highest precision at 0.889 among individual models. This suggests that averaging many decorrelated trees provides better generalization in this feature space than sequential boosting, even though boosting has higher representational flexibility.

The Super Learner at ROC-AUC 0.960 achieves the highest overall discrimination, only marginally above random forest at 0.959. This difference is negligible in practical terms and consistent with the earlier finding that the Super Learner assigns full weight to SL.ranger, meaning both models effectively learn the same boundary. The slight advantage in ROC-AUC likely reflects improved probability calibration from stacking rather than a substantively different decision surface. In contrast, random forest shows a slightly higher accuracy of 0.894 compared to the Super Learner at 0.893, illustrating that small divergences between ranking quality and thresholded classification can occur even when underlying models are nearly identical.

Across all models, recall is consistently higher than precision by a small margin. This means models tend to produce slightly more false positives than false negatives, or in substantive terms, more alternative songs incorrectly classified as maximalist than the reverse. The consistency of this pattern across all six models suggests it is a property of the feature space rather than any specific algorithm, consistent with the directional error structure discussed in Section 8.

Taken together, these results provide a coherent answer to the central question. Production aesthetic is strongly predictable from Spotify audio features, with performance reaching ROC-AUC levels around 0.96 and accuracy close to 90 percent even without relying on loudness driven artifacts. The gap between logistic regression and the best models quantifies the contribution of non-linear structure at roughly 7 percentage points in ROC-AUC, showing that aesthetic signals are encoded not only in additive feature effects but also in interactions and threshold based structure within the audio feature space.

## 8.2 ROC Curves

```{r roc-curves, fig.cap="Figure 4. ROC curves for all six models on the test set.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
roc_list <- list(
  "Logistic Regression" = roc(
    as.numeric(lr_preds$truth    == "Maximalist"),
    lr_preds$.pred_prob,    quiet = TRUE),
  "Lasso"               = roc(
    as.numeric(lasso_preds$truth == "Maximalist"),
    lasso_preds$.pred_prob, quiet = TRUE),
  "Decision Tree"       = roc(
    as.numeric(dt_preds$truth    == "Maximalist"),
    dt_preds$.pred_prob,    quiet = TRUE),
  "Random Forest"       = roc(
    as.numeric(rf_preds$truth    == "Maximalist"),
    rf_preds$.pred_prob,    quiet = TRUE),
  "XGBoost"             = roc(
    as.numeric(xgb_preds$truth   == "Maximalist"),
    xgb_preds$.pred_prob,   quiet = TRUE),
  "Super Learner"       = roc(
    as.numeric(sl_preds$truth    == "Maximalist"),
    sl_preds$.pred_prob,    quiet = TRUE)
)

roc_df <- map_dfr(names(roc_list), function(nm) {
  r <- roc_list[[nm]]
  tibble(
    model       = nm,
    specificity = r$specificities,
    sensitivity = r$sensitivities,
    auc         = as.numeric(r$auc)
  )
}) %>%
  mutate(
    model_label = paste0(model, " (AUC = ", round(auc, 3), ")"),
    model_label = factor(model_label)
  )

ggplot(roc_df, aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity,
                   color = model_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curves — All Models (Test Set)",
    x     = "1 − Specificity (False Positive Rate)",
    y     = "Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)",
    color = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.direction = "vertical",
    legend.box       = "vertical",
    legend.justification = "left")
```

The ROC curves plot the tradeoff between sensitivity, the true positive rate for maximalist songs, and the false positive rate for alternative songs, across all possible decision thresholds. Each point on a curve corresponds to a specific probability threshold applied to the model’s predicted probabilities, and the area under the curve summarizes this full tradeoff into a single measure of ranking performance. A model that perfectly separates the two classes would rise directly from the origin to the top left corner and then across the top edge, yielding an AUC of 1.0. The dashed diagonal line represents random classification performance, with an AUC of 0.5.

The most informative region of the ROC space is the upper left corner, where false positive rates are low and small differences between models become most visible. In this region, the Super Learner and Random Forest curves rise most sharply and maintain the highest sensitivity at low false positive rates, indicating the strongest ability to correctly identify maximalist songs while avoiding misclassification of alternative songs. XGBoost follows closely, with a curve that remains below the top two ensemble methods but clearly above the remaining models across this low false positive region. The Decision Tree sits below XGBoost but above the linear models, consistent with its intermediate AUC of 0.904 and its position as a partially non-linear model.

Logistic Regression and Lasso produce nearly identical ROC curves across the entire range of thresholds, confirming what the AUC values and bootstrap intervals already suggest, that both models learn the same linear decision boundary. At higher false positive rates, where the threshold becomes more permissive and most observations are labeled as maximalist, all models converge in sensitivity and the curves move closer together. The meaningful separation between models therefore occurs primarily in the low false positive region, which is also the most relevant operating range for most practical classification settings.

This representation is especially relevant for potential deployment settings described earlier in the study. A streaming platform would not necessarily operate at the default 0.5 threshold, and the ROC curves show how performance changes as that threshold shifts. If minimizing false positives is more important than maximizing sensitivity, the operating point would move toward the left side of the curve. Across all such operating regions, the Super Learner and Random Forest consistently provide the most favorable balance between sensitivity and false positive rate, which is consistent with their top performance in ROC-AUC and explains why ROC-AUC is used as the primary evaluation metric throughout the analysis.

## 8.3 Bootstrap confidence intervals around ROC-AUC

Point estimates alone do not convey uncertainty. Two models whose AUC point estimates differ by 0.002 may not be meaningfully different if their confidence intervals overlap substantially. Bootstrapping resamples the test set with replacement B times, recomputes AUC each time, and takes the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the resulting distribution as the 95% CI. This makes no distributional assumptions about AUC and is appropriate for comparing non-nested models.

```{r bootstrap-confidence-intervals}
boot_auc_ci <- function(preds_tbl, B = 1000, seed = 42) {
  set.seed(seed)
  n <- nrow(preds_tbl)
  auc_boot <- numeric(B)
  y_true <- as.numeric(preds_tbl$truth == "Maximalist")

  for (i in seq_len(B)) {
    idx <- sample(n, n, replace = TRUE)
    boot_y    <- y_true[idx]
    boot_prob <- preds_tbl$.pred_prob[idx]

    # Skip degenerate bootstrap samples with only one class
    if (length(unique(boot_y)) < 2) {
      auc_boot[i] <- NA_real_
      next
    }
    auc_boot[i] <- as.numeric(
      roc(boot_y, boot_prob, quiet = TRUE)$auc
    )
  }

  auc_boot <- auc_boot[!is.na(auc_boot)]
  quantile(auc_boot, c(0.025, 0.975))
}

cat("Computing bootstrap AUC confidence intervals (B = 1000)...\n")

# Named list of prediction tibbles — order matches all_metrics
pred_list <- list(
  "Super Learner"        = sl_preds,
  "Random Forest"        = rf_preds,
  "XGBoost"              = xgb_preds,
  "Decision Tree"        = dt_preds,
  "Logistic Regression"  = lr_preds,
  "Lasso"                = lasso_preds
)

ci_results <- map_dfr(names(pred_list), function(nm) {
  ci <- boot_auc_ci(pred_list[[nm]])
  tibble(
    Model   = nm,
    AUC     = round(roc(
      as.numeric(pred_list[[nm]]$truth == "Maximalist"),
      pred_list[[nm]]$.pred_prob,
      quiet = TRUE
    )$auc, 4),
    CI_lower = round(ci[1], 4),
    CI_upper = round(ci[2], 4)
  )
}) %>%
  arrange(desc(AUC))

cat("\nROC-AUC with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (test set):\n")
print(ci_results)
```

```{r ci-visualization, fig.cap="Figure 5. ROC-AUC with 95% Bootstrap Confidence Intervals (Test Set).", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
ci_results %>%
  mutate(
    Model = factor(Model, levels = rev(ci_results$Model))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = AUC, y = Model, color = Model)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbarh(
    aes(xmin = CI_lower, xmax = CI_upper),
    height = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(AUC, " [", CI_lower, ", ", CI_upper, "]")),
    hjust = -0.08, size = 3.2
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(
      min(ci_results$CI_lower) - 0.02,
      max(ci_results$CI_upper) + 0.08
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "ROC-AUC with 95% Bootstrap Confidence Intervals (Test Set)",
    subtitle = "B = 1000 bootstrap resamples of the test set",
    x        = "ROC-AUC",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

The bootstrap confidence interval plot adds an important layer of statistical evidence to the point estimates in the performance summary table. Each interval is constructed from 1000 bootstrap resamples of the test set, using the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the resampled AUC distribution as the bounds. This approach does not rely on distributional assumptions for AUC and directly captures uncertainty arising from the finite size of the test set.

The most important feature of the plot is the clear separation between the confidence intervals of models across the performance ranking. The Super Learner interval of 0.9587 to 0.963 does not overlap with the Random Forest interval of 0.9576 to 0.9619, although the overlap is extremely small, confirming that the Super Learner’s slight advantage in point estimate AUC is stable across resamples but substantively minimal. More importantly, neither the Super Learner nor the Random Forest interval overlaps with the XGBoost interval of 0.9422 to 0.9477, indicating that the performance gap between ensemble methods and gradient boosting is not due to sampling variation in the test set.

The separation between XGBoost and the Decision Tree is also clear. XGBoost’s lower bound of 0.9422 is well above the Decision Tree’s upper bound of 0.9079, confirming that sequential residual fitting captures substantially more signal than a single recursive partition across all bootstrap samples. The Decision Tree interval of 0.9 to 0.9079 does not overlap with XGBoost above or the linear models below, placing it firmly as an intermediate model in the complexity gradient rather than one whose ranking depends on test set sampling noise.

Logistic Regression and Lasso produce identical point estimates and identical confidence intervals of 0.8876 to 0.896, with complete overlap. This confirms the earlier finding that both models learn the same linear boundary and that regularization at the optimal level does not materially change test set performance. The linear ceiling of approximately 0.89 AUC is therefore a stable and well identified benchmark rather than an estimate sensitive to sampling variation.

Taken together, the confidence intervals show that the performance ordering in the summary table is statistically stable. Each model occupies a distinct position in the hierarchy, with only minimal proximity between the Super Learner and Random Forest. The overall gap between the linear ceiling at about 0.89 AUC and the best ensemble methods at about 0.96 AUC is robust across bootstrap samples and reflects a genuine difference in predictive capacity rather than random variation in the test set.

## 8.4 Calibration Plots

Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities correspond to observed outcome frequencies. A well-calibrated model that assigns a 70% probability to maximalist songs should be correct about 70% of the time among those predictions. The diagonal line represents perfect calibration. Points above the diagonal indicate underprediction of maximalism, meaning the true frequency is higher than the predicted probability, while points below the diagonal indicate overprediction. Calibration matters in the streaming platform setting because the raw probability values, not only the final class labels, may directly influence ranking and recommendation strength.

```{r calibration-plots, fig.cap="Figure 6. Calibration plots for all six models.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
all_preds <- bind_rows(
  lr_preds    %>% mutate(model = "Logistic Regression"),
  lasso_preds %>% mutate(model = "Lasso"),
  dt_preds    %>% mutate(model = "Decision Tree"),
  rf_preds    %>% mutate(model = "Random Forest"),
  xgb_preds   %>% mutate(model = "XGBoost"),
  sl_preds    %>% mutate(model = "Super Learner")
) %>%
  mutate(
    y_numeric = as.numeric(truth == "Maximalist"),
    model     = factor(model, levels = c(
      "Logistic Regression", "Lasso", "Decision Tree",
      "Random Forest", "XGBoost", "Super Learner"
    ))
  )

calibration_df <- all_preds %>%
  group_by(model) %>%
  mutate(bin = cut(.pred_prob,
                   breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.1),
                   include.lowest = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(model, bin) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_pred = mean(.pred_prob),
    obs_rate  = mean(y_numeric),
    n         = n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

ggplot(calibration_df, aes(x = mean_pred, y = obs_rate, color = model)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n), alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 5), guide = "none") +
  
  # Control breaks with cleaner single-decimal labels
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1), 
                     labels = c("0.0", "0.25", "0.5", "0.75", "1.0")) +
  
  facet_wrap(~model, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title    = "Calibration Plots - All Models (Test Set)",
    subtitle = "Diagonal = perfect calibration",
    x        = "Mean Predicted Probability of Maximalist",
    y        = "Observed Maximalist Rate",
    color    = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(1.5, "lines") 
  )
```

Calibration measures whether a model’s predicted probabilities can be interpreted as reliable probability statements rather than only as ranking scores. A perfectly calibrated model that assigns 0.7 probability to maximalist songs should be correct about 70 percent of the time among all songs receiving that score. The diagonal line in each panel represents perfect calibration, and deviations from this line indicate systematic overprediction or underprediction in specific probability ranges. Calibration is different from discrimination. A model can rank songs well and achieve high ROC-AUC while still producing poorly calibrated probabilities that are not trustworthy as absolute risk estimates. For the streaming platform context where predicted probabilities may directly inform recommendation strength, calibration quality is an additional requirement beyond ranking performance.

Logistic regression and Lasso show the strongest calibration performance in the entire pipeline. Their curves closely follow the diagonal across most of the probability range from 0 to 1, with only small deviations at the extremes where fewer observations are available in each bin. This behavior is expected because logistic regression directly optimizes log-loss, which penalizes miscalibrated probability estimates and tends to produce well-calibrated outputs when the model is correctly specified. The nearly identical calibration curves for these two models further confirm that Lasso does not meaningfully alter the underlying probability structure learned by logistic regression.

The decision tree displays a staircase pattern rather than a smooth curve, with visible jumps between discrete probability levels. This is a direct consequence of how trees generate probabilities. Each leaf node assigns the same probability to all observations that fall into it, equal to the proportion of maximalist songs in that leaf during training. As a result, the model can only output a finite set of probability values determined by the number of leaves, which produces the stepped structure in the calibration plot. Despite this discreteness, calibration is reasonably accurate in the middle probability range, with larger deviations appearing near the extremes.

Random forest shows a consistent S shaped deviation from the diagonal. At low predicted probabilities, the curve sits above the diagonal, indicating slight overprediction of maximalism for songs that are actually less likely to be maximalist. In the middle range, the curve is closer to the diagonal, while at higher predicted probabilities it bends below the diagonal, reflecting overconfidence in high probability predictions. This pattern is a known characteristic of averaging many decision trees, where each tree tends to produce extreme leaf probabilities and the averaging process compresses predictions toward the center. The result is strong ranking performance but imperfect probability calibration.

XGBoost shows noticeably better calibration than random forest across most of the probability range. Its curve tracks the diagonal more closely through the middle region and only shows stronger deviation at the high probability end, where a cluster of predictions near 1 corresponds to songs that are highly but not perfectly likely to be maximalist. This improvement is consistent with XGBoost’s direct optimization of log-loss during training, which penalizes probability errors more directly than the impurity-based splitting criteria used in tree construction methods.

The Super Learner shows the closest alignment with the diagonal overall, particularly in the central probability range between 0.25 and 0.75 where most decision-making uncertainty lies. This reflects the effect of stacking, where out-of-fold predictions from the best-performing base learner are combined in a way that minimizes cross-validated log-loss. The result is a correction of the systematic miscalibration seen in standalone tree ensembles, producing probability estimates that more closely match observed frequencies. This improved calibration, combined with near top-tier discrimination, explains why the Super Learner is the most reliable model for applications where probability values themselves are used, not only class labels.

## 8.5 Density plot of predicted probabilities by true outcome label

For each model, predicted probabilities are plotted separately for songs whose true label is Alternative (0) and Maximalist (1). A well-discriminating model produces two clearly separated density curves. Alternative songs should cluster near p = 0 and Maximalist songs should cluster near p = 1. Overlap between the two curves corresponds directly to the model's error region. The shape of each curve should also be informative. A sharp peak near 0 or 1 would indicate confident correct predictions, while a broad or flat distribution would indicate the model is uncertain across many songs in that class. Comparing across models would show how discrimination improves as model complexity increases.

```{r density-plot, fig.cap="Figure 7. Density plot of predicted probabilities by true outcome label.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
all_preds %>%
  mutate(
    true_label = if_else(
      truth == "Maximalist",
      "True: Maximalist (1)",
      "True: Alternative (0)"
    ),
    model = factor(model, levels = c(
      "Logistic Regression", "Lasso", "Decision Tree",
      "Random Forest", "XGBoost", "Super Learner"
    ))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(
    x    = .pred_prob,
    fill = true_label,
    color = true_label
  )) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ model, ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "True: Alternative (0)" = "steelblue",
      "True: Maximalist (1)"  = "coral"
    )
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "True: Alternative (0)" = "steelblue",
      "True: Maximalist (1)"  = "coral"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1),
    labels = c("0", "0.25", "0.5", "0.75", "1")
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0.5,
    linetype   = "dashed",
    color      = "grey40",
    linewidth  = 0.6
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted Probability Distributions by True Outcome Label",
    subtitle = paste(
      "Each curve shows the density of predicted P (Maximalist) for",
      "songs of that true class.",
      "\nCurves closer to 0 and 1 with less overlap indicate better discrimination.",
      "\nDashed line = 0.5 decision threshold."
    ),
    x     = "Predicted Probability of Maximalist",
    y     = "Density",
    fill  = "True Label",
    color = "True Label"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.title     = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

The plot consists of separate panels for each model, and each panel shows two density curves representing the distribution of predicted probabilities. The blue curve corresponds to songs whose true label is Alternative, and it shows where those songs are placed on the model’s predicted probability scale. The orange curve corresponds to songs whose true label is Maximalist, showing the predicted probabilities assigned to those songs. The vertical dashed line at 0.5 marks the decision threshold used to convert probabilities into class labels.

Across models, Logistic Regression and Lasso show substantial overlap between the two distributions. A large number of truly Alternative songs receive predicted probabilities in the mid range, roughly between 0.3 and 0.6, while many truly Maximalist songs also fall in a similar range, roughly between 0.4 and 0.7. This indicates that the model is uncertain for a large portion of observations, with weak separation between the two classes and no clear bimodal structure.

The Decision Tree shows a different pattern. The distribution for Maximalist songs becomes visibly spiky and irregular, which reflects the discrete nature of tree-based predictions. Because a decision tree assigns a fixed probability to each terminal leaf, the resulting density is composed of a limited set of probability levels rather than a smooth curve. This produces a jagged appearance and indicates improved separation relative to the linear models, but still with clear limitations in smooth probabilistic discrimination.

Random Forest and the Super Learner show the strongest separation between the two classes. The blue distribution for Alternative songs is concentrated near zero, indicating high confidence in classifying these songs correctly as Alternative. The orange distribution for Maximalist songs is concentrated near one, indicating similarly high confidence for Maximalist classification. The overlap in the middle region is substantially reduced compared to earlier models, producing a clear bimodal structure where most songs cluster near the two extremes.

XGBoost follows a similar pattern to Random Forest but with slightly more mass in the overlapping middle region. This corresponds to its somewhat lower AUC relative to Random Forest and the Super Learner, and reflects less extreme probability separation between the two classes.

The overall progression from Logistic Regression to Decision Tree to Random Forest and Super Learner visually matches the performance improvements observed in the AUC results. As model complexity increases, predictions shift away from the uncertain middle region toward more confident extremes near zero and one. The remaining overlap even in the strongest models corresponds to a subset of songs that are consistently difficult to classify across all methods, indicating that they occupy a genuinely ambiguous region in the audio feature space rather than being artifacts of model choice.

## 8.6 Confusion Matrix Heatmaps

```{r confusion-matrices, fig.cap="Figure 8. Confusion matrix heatmaps for all six models.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
all_preds %>%
  mutate(
    pred_class = factor(
      if_else(.pred_prob >= 0.5, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
      levels = c("Alternative", "Maximalist")
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(model) %>%
  count(truth, pred_class) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pred_class, y = truth, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)),
            size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~model, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrices - All Models (Test Set)",
    x     = "Predicted Class",
    y     = "True Class",
    fill  = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

The confusion matrices translate abstract performance metrics into concrete counts of correct and incorrect classifications on the 21,576 song test set, making the practical meaning of each model’s performance directly visible. Each matrix has four cells. The top left shows maximalist songs correctly predicted as maximalist. The top right shows maximalist songs incorrectly predicted as alternative. The bottom left shows alternative songs correctly predicted as alternative. The bottom right shows alternative songs incorrectly predicted as maximalist. The off-diagonal cells represent errors and reveal both the scale and direction of each model’s mistakes.

Logistic regression and Lasso produce nearly identical error patterns. Logistic regression makes 1,739 maximalist-to-alternative errors and 2,382 alternative-to-maximalist errors, while Lasso makes 1,751 and 2,376 respectively. The asymmetry is consistent across both models, with roughly 37 percent more alternative-to-maximalist errors than maximalist-to-alternative errors. Because this pattern appears in both linear models, it reflects the structure of the linear decision boundary rather than a model-specific artifact. When uncertainty arises near the boundary, the linear feature space tends to push ambiguous songs toward the maximalist side more often than toward the alternative side.

The decision tree reduces total errors, with 1,529 maximalist-to-alternative errors and 2,114 alternative-to-maximalist errors. The same directional asymmetry remains, showing that this imbalance is not specific to linear models but is embedded in the feature space itself. The improvement is spread across both error types rather than concentrated in one direction, suggesting the tree is learning a more accurate boundary overall rather than correcting only one type of mistake.

Random forest further reduces errors to 1,038 maximalist-to-alternative and 1,253 alternative-to-maximalist, giving the lowest total error count among all models at 2,291. The asymmetry narrows compared to the linear models, with a ratio of about 1.21 instead of 1.37. This indicates that the non-linear boundary learned by random forest is better at correctly identifying alternative songs near the decision boundary that linear models tend to misclassify as maximalist.

XGBoost produces 1,155 maximalist-to-alternative errors and 1,499 alternative-to-maximalist errors, with a directional ratio of about 1.30. The Super Learner achieves 1,020 maximalist-to-alternative errors and 1,279 alternative-to-maximalist errors, for a total of 2,299 errors. Its pattern closely matches random forest, reinforcing that both models are learning very similar decision boundaries, with only minor differences in how errors are distributed.

The persistent asymmetry across all models, where alternative-to-maximalist errors consistently exceed maximalist-to-alternative errors, is a substantive structural finding. It suggests that the feature space encodes maximalist songs in a more compact and easily identifiable region, while alternative songs occupy a more diffuse region with fuzzier boundaries. As a result, borderline songs are more often pulled toward the maximalist class under uncertainty. This has direct implications for deployment, since any real-world threshold choice will likely produce more false maximalist predictions than false alternative ones, and operating decisions should account for that imbalance.

## 8.7 Performance Summary and Interpretation

```{r performance-summary, fig.cap="Figure 9. Model comparison across all performance metrics and directional error rates.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
# Build directional error rates to combine with performance metrics
error_dir <- all_preds %>%
  mutate(
    pred_class = factor(
      if_else(.pred_prob >= 0.5, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
      levels = c("Alternative", "Maximalist")
    ),
    error_type = case_when(
      truth == "Alternative" & pred_class == "Maximalist" ~ "Alt→Max",
      truth == "Maximalist"  & pred_class == "Alternative" ~ "Max→Alt",
      TRUE ~ "Correct"
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(model) %>%
  summarise(
    alt_as_max_rate = mean(error_type == "Alt→Max"),
    max_as_alt_rate = mean(error_type == "Max→Alt"),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rename(Model = model)

perf_df <- all_metrics %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Model, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(metric = recode(metric,
    roc_auc   = "ROC-AUC",
    accuracy  = "Accuracy",
    f_meas    = "F1-Score",
    precision = "Precision",
    recall    = "Recall"
  ))

error_dir_df <- error_dir %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Model, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(metric = recode(metric,
    alt_as_max_rate = "Alt→Max Error",
    max_as_alt_rate = "Max→Alt Error"
  ))

bind_rows(perf_df, error_dir_df) %>%
  mutate(
    Model  = factor(Model, levels = c(
      "Logistic Regression", "Lasso", "Decision Tree",
      "Random Forest", "XGBoost", "Super Learner"
    )),
    metric = factor(metric, levels = c(
      "ROC-AUC", "Accuracy", "F1-Score",
      "Precision", "Recall",
      "Alt→Max Error", "Max→Alt Error"
    ))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Model, y = value, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(value, 3)), vjust = -0.3, size = 2.8) +
  facet_wrap(~metric, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Model Comparison: Performance and Directional Errors",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

The model comparison figure consolidates all performance metrics and directional error rates into a single view, enabling simultaneous comparison across all six models on seven dimensions. Reading across the panels reveals patterns that appear separately in earlier tables and plots but become clearer when viewed side by side.

The ROC-AUC, accuracy, and F1-score panels show a consistent performance gradient. The two linear models appear together at the lower end of each panel with identical values, the decision tree sits modestly higher, and random forest and the Super Learner reach the top, with XGBoost in between. The fact that this ordering is preserved across discrimination, overall correctness, and the balance between precision and recall indicates that the performance differences are not driven by a single metric but reflect a consistent improvement in classification ability as model complexity increases.

The precision and recall panels show how these improvements are distributed across error types. Precision, which measures how often a maximalist prediction is correct, increases from 0.796 in the linear models to 0.889 in random forest, a gain of 9.3 percentage points. Recall, which measures how often actual maximalist songs are correctly identified, increases from 0.842 to 0.906, a gain of 6.4 percentage points. This indicates that the non-linear models improve precision more strongly than recall relative to the linear baseline, meaning they are especially effective at reducing false maximalist predictions. The Super Learner shows precision of 0.887 and recall of 0.908, slightly lower precision but slightly higher recall than random forest, showing that the two models operate with nearly identical performance but distribute errors slightly differently at the default threshold.

The directional error panels provide the most substantively informative view. The alternative-to-maximalist error rate is highest in the linear models at about 11 percent, decreases to about 9.8 percent in the decision tree, and drops further to roughly 5.8 to 6.9 percent in the ensemble models. The maximalist-to-alternative error rate follows the same pattern, decreasing from about 8.1 percent in the linear models to around 7.1 percent in the decision tree and to roughly 4.7 to 5.4 percent in the ensemble methods.

Two patterns stand out. First, the alternative-to-maximalist error rate is consistently higher than the maximalist-to-alternative error rate across all models. This confirms that the directional asymmetry observed in the confusion matrices is not tied to a specific model or threshold but is a structural property of the feature space. Second, the gap between the two error types narrows as model complexity increases. In the linear models, alternative-to-maximalist errors are about 36 percent higher, while in the Super Learner the gap narrows to about 25 percent. This suggests that more flexible models reduce but do not eliminate the tendency to classify ambiguous songs as maximalist, because part of this asymmetry is embedded in how the audio feature space represents the two aesthetic categories.

---

# 9 Error Analysis

## 9.1 Directional Error Summary

```{r error-analysis}
collect_errors <- function(preds_tbl, model_name) {
  preds_tbl %>%
    mutate(
      .pred_class = factor(
        if_else(.pred_prob >= 0.5, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
        levels = levels(truth)
      ),
      error = truth != .pred_class,
      error_type = case_when(
        truth == "Alternative" & .pred_class == "Maximalist" ~ "Alt→Max",
        truth == "Maximalist"  & .pred_class == "Alternative" ~ "Max→Alt",
        TRUE ~ "Correct"
      ),
      model = model_name
    )
}

lr_err    <- collect_errors(lr_preds,    "Logistic Regression")
lasso_err <- collect_errors(lasso_preds, "Lasso")
dt_err    <- collect_errors(dt_preds,    "Decision Tree")
rf_err    <- collect_errors(rf_preds,    "Random Forest")
xgb_err   <- collect_errors(xgb_preds,   "XGBoost")
sl_err    <- collect_errors(sl_preds,    "Super Learner")

all_errors <- bind_rows(
  lr_err, lasso_err, dt_err,
  rf_err, xgb_err, sl_err
) %>%
  mutate(model = factor(model, levels = c(
    "Logistic Regression", "Lasso", "Decision Tree",
    "Random Forest", "XGBoost", "Super Learner"
  )))

error_summary <- all_errors %>%
  group_by(model) %>%
  summarise(
    total_errors     = sum(error),
    total_error_rate = mean(error),
    alt_as_max       = sum(error_type == "Alt→Max"),
    alt_as_max_rate  = mean(error_type == "Alt→Max"),
    max_as_alt       = sum(error_type == "Max→Alt"),
    max_as_alt_rate  = mean(error_type == "Max→Alt"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Wrong predictions on test set:\n")
error_summary %>%
  mutate(out = paste0(
    "  ", str_pad(as.character(model), 22, "right"),
    ": ", total_errors, " tracks (",
    scales::percent(total_error_rate, accuracy = 0.01), ")"
  )) %>%
  pull(out) %>%
  cat(sep = "\n")

cat("\nDirectional errors:\n")
error_summary %>%
  mutate(out = paste0(
    "  ", str_pad(as.character(model), 22, "right"),
    ": Alternative → Maximalist = ", str_pad(alt_as_max, 4, "left"),
    " (", scales::percent(alt_as_max_rate, accuracy = 0.01), ")",
    "   Maximalist → Alternative = ", str_pad(max_as_alt, 4, "left"),
    " (", scales::percent(max_as_alt_rate, accuracy = 0.01), ")"
  )) %>%
  pull(out) %>%
  cat(sep = "\n")
```

The directional error breakdown translates aggregate error rates into a more informative accounting of where each model fails and in which direction. Total errors decrease steadily from 4,125 tracks for logistic regression at 19.12 percent to 2,254 tracks for random forest at 10.45 percent, with the Super Learner at 2,242 tracks and 10.39 percent essentially matching random forest. The decision tree at 3,510 tracks and 16.27 percent sits closer to the linear models than to the ensemble methods, confirming that a single tree captures some non-linear structure but cannot approach the error reduction achieved by aggregating many trees.

The directional breakdown shows that every model makes more alternative-to-maximalist errors than maximalist-to-alternative errors. In logistic regression, 2,369 alternative songs are incorrectly predicted as maximalist compared to 1,756 maximalist songs incorrectly predicted as alternative, a ratio of 1.37. Lasso is nearly identical at 2,378 versus 1,747, a ratio of 1.36. The decision tree narrows this only slightly to 2,114 versus 1,529, a ratio of 1.38, indicating that recursive partitioning reduces overall error but does not change the underlying directional pattern. The ensemble methods show a more noticeable reduction in the imbalance. Random forest produces 1,265 alternative-to-maximalist errors versus 989 maximalist-to-alternative errors, a ratio of 1.21. The Super Learner produces 1,280 versus 962, a ratio of 1.25. XGBoost sits in between at 1,515 versus 1,106, a ratio of 1.30.

The persistence of this asymmetry across all six models, including the most flexible ensembles, indicates that it is a structural property of the audio feature space rather than a limitation of any single modeling approach. The maximalist aesthetic forms a more compact and coherent region of the feature space, with high energy and low acousticness producing a dense cluster, while the alternative aesthetic is more diffuse and spread across a wider range of feature combinations. As a result, songs near the boundary of the alternative region are more likely to be pulled into the maximalist cluster than the reverse, producing the consistent directional imbalance.

The gradual reduction of the ratio from about 1.37 in the linear models to about 1.21 in random forest suggests that non-linear models better capture the boundary structure of the alternative region, even though they do not eliminate the asymmetry entirely. The remaining imbalance in the strongest models reflects a genuine property of how the two aesthetics are represented in the feature space.

For deployment, this asymmetry has a direct operational implication. At the default 0.5 threshold, all models produce more false maximalist predictions than false alternative predictions. If the costs of these errors differ in practice, for example if misclassifying alternative songs as maximalist is more undesirable than the reverse, then the decision threshold should be adjusted above 0.5 to reduce that error type. The ROC curves in Section 7.2 show how sensitivity and false positive rate change across thresholds, while the directional error rates here make clear what is being exchanged when that threshold is shifted.

## 9.2 Error Overlap Analysis

```{r error-overlap}
overlap_df <- tibble(
  row_idx    = seq_len(nrow(test_data)),
  lr_error   = lr_err$error,
  lasso_error = lasso_err$error,
  dt_error   = dt_err$error,
  rf_error   = rf_err$error,
  xgb_error  = xgb_err$error,
  sl_error   = sl_err$error,
  truth      = lr_err$truth
) %>%
  mutate(n_models_wrong = lr_error + lasso_error + dt_error +
           rf_error + xgb_error + sl_error)

hard_idx   <- overlap_df %>% filter(n_models_wrong == 6) %>% pull(row_idx)
easy_idx   <- overlap_df %>% filter(n_models_wrong == 0) %>% pull(row_idx)
hard_cases <- test_data[hard_idx, ]
easy_cases <- test_data[easy_idx, ]

cat("Error overlap analysis:\n")
cat("  Correctly classified by all 6 models     :",
    nrow(easy_cases), "\n")
cat("  Hard errors — all 6 models wrong         :",
    nrow(hard_cases), "\n\n")

model_cols  <- c("lr_error", "lasso_error", "dt_error",
                 "rf_error", "xgb_error", "sl_error")
model_names <- c("LR", "Lasso", "DT", "RF", "XGB", "SL")

cat("Errors shared between model pairs (both wrong on same observation):\n")
for (i in 1:(length(model_cols) - 1)) {
  for (j in (i + 1):length(model_cols)) {
    shared <- sum(overlap_df[[model_cols[i]]] &
                    overlap_df[[model_cols[j]]])
    cat("  ", str_pad(
      paste0(model_names[i], " + ", model_names[j]),
      12, "right"), ":", shared, "\n")
  }
}

cat("\nTrue class of universally misclassified songs:\n")
hard_class <- table(hard_cases$production_aesthetic)
cat("  Alternative :", hard_class["Alternative"], "\n")
cat("  Maximalist  :", hard_class["Maximalist"],  "\n")
```

The error overlap analysis asks whether different models are making mistakes on the same songs or on different parts of the test set. If two models fail on largely the same observations, they are likely capturing similar structure in the data and sharing the same blind spots. If their errors are more distinct, then combining models has the potential to reduce total error by correcting different failure modes.

The pairwise overlap table shows a clear structure in how models agree and disagree. Logistic regression and Lasso share 4,095 errors on the same songs, which is almost the entirety of their respective error sets. This near-complete overlap confirms that the two models are effectively learning the same decision boundary and failing on the same observations.

The overlap between the linear models and the decision tree is around 2,650 songs, meaning roughly 65 percent of the linear models’ errors are also made by the tree. This suggests that the decision tree resolves some cases that are difficult for linear models, but still fails on many of the same borderline songs.

The overlap between the linear models and the ensemble methods is substantially lower. Logistic regression and random forest share about 1,883 errors, meaning random forest misclassifies only about 46 percent of the songs that logistic regression gets wrong. The remaining cases are correctly classified by random forest, showing that non-linear structure resolves a large portion of errors that linear models cannot handle. The overlap between random forest and the Super Learner is higher at 2,113, which is expected given that the Super Learner is dominated by the random forest learner and behaves very similarly in practice.

The most important finding is the existence of a hard core of 1463 songs that all six models misclassify. These observations define a practical limit of what can be learned from the available audio features. The true labels of these songs are not balanced between 908 alternative and 555 maximalist. This reveals a systematic asymmetry in which alternative tracks are harder to classify than maximalist tracks across all six models. This could be explained by the sociological theory that countercultural aesthetic categories defined by restraint, thus having more diffuse sonic boundaries than categories defined by positive sonic prescriptions.

---

# 10 Feature Importance and Substantive Interpretation

## 10.1 Extract Importance from All Models

```{r importance-extraction}
# Logistic Regression: coefficient magnitude with direction
lr_imp_df <- lr_fit %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  transmute(
    feature    = term,
    importance = abs(estimate),
    direction  = if_else(estimate > 0, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
    model      = "Logistic Regression (|Coef|)"
  )

# Lasso: non-zero coefficients only, with direction
lasso_imp_df <- lasso_final %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(estimate != 0, term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  transmute(
    feature    = term,
    importance = abs(estimate),
    direction  = if_else(estimate > 0, "Maximalist", "Alternative"),
    model      = "Lasso (|Coef|)"
  )

# Decision Tree: impurity reduction importance — no sign because
# tree splits are nonparametric and a feature's effect direction
# can vary across different branches of the tree
dt_imp_df <- dt_final %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip::vi() %>%
  transmute(
    feature   = Variable,
    importance = Importance,
    direction  = NA_character_,
    model      = "Decision Tree (Split Importance)"
  )

# Random Forest: permutation importance — no sign because
# a feature's effect direction depends on its value and its
# interactions with other features
rf_imp_df <- vi(extract_fit_parsnip(rf_final)) %>%
  transmute(
    feature    = Variable,
    importance = Importance,
    direction  = NA_character_,
    model      = "Random Forest (Permutation)"
  )

# XGBoost: gain-based importance — no sign for the same reason
xgb_imp_df <- vi(extract_fit_parsnip(xgb_final)) %>%
  transmute(
    feature    = Variable,
    importance = Importance,
    direction  = NA_character_,
    model      = "XGBoost (Gain)"
  )
```

## 10.2 Printed Feature Importance Summaries

```{r importance-print}
cat("=== Feature Importance: Top 10 per Model ===\n\n")

for (imp_df in list(lr_imp_df, lasso_imp_df, dt_imp_df,
                    rf_imp_df, xgb_imp_df)) {
  cat(unique(imp_df$model), ":\n")
  imp_df %>%
    arrange(desc(importance)) %>%
    slice_head(n = 10) %>%
    mutate(
      importance = round(importance, 4),
      dir_label  = if_else(is.na(direction), "no sign", direction),
      out        = paste0(
        "  ", str_pad(feature, 30, "right"),
        ": ", str_pad(importance, 8, "left"),
        "  (", dir_label, ")"
      )
    ) %>%
    pull(out) %>%
    cat(sep = "\n")
  cat("\n")
}
```

The feature importance tables reveal both the consistent signal structure that all models agree on and the interpretive differences that arise from each model’s distinct notion of importance. Before comparing models, it is important to note that the importance values are not on a shared scale and should not be compared in magnitude across models. Logistic regression and Lasso report absolute coefficient magnitudes on the log-odds scale. The decision tree reports total Gini impurity reduction accumulated across all splits where a feature is used, which produces large values at this sample size. Random forest reports permutation importance as the average drop in out-of-bag accuracy when a feature is randomly shuffled. XGBoost reports average gain per split, aggregated across all trees. These definitions measure different quantities, so meaningful comparison is limited to rank ordering rather than raw values.

Within each model, the rankings are highly consistent. Energy is the top feature in every model without exception, appearing first in logistic regression, Lasso, the decision tree, random forest, and XGBoost. This agreement across fundamentally different importance definitions is strong evidence that energy is the primary audio correlate of production aesthetic in this dataset, consistent with the interpretation of maximalist production as high intensity and high compression. In the decision tree, energy is especially dominant, with total impurity reduction of 18,663, more than twice that of acousticness at 7,923, indicating that early splits in the tree are driven primarily by energy thresholds.

Acousticness shows the most important divergence across models. It does not appear in the top 10 of the linear models despite being one of the strongest correlates in the exploratory analysis. In contrast, it ranks near the top in all tree-based models, including second in the decision tree and third in both random forest and XGBoost. This difference reflects multicollinearity between energy and acousticness. In a linear model, shared variance between correlated predictors is absorbed primarily by one variable, here energy. In tree-based models, where splits evaluate one feature at a time, acousticness can still contribute independently once energy has already been used.

Instrumentalness remains consistently important across all models, ranking second in the linear models, second in XGBoost, third in random forest, and fourth in the decision tree. This stability indicates that even though most songs have values near zero, the small subset of truly instrumental tracks carries meaningful signal for distinguishing alternative aesthetics. Duration appears among the top features in the linear models and ensemble methods but not in the decision tree, suggesting that its effect is weaker and more distributed across interactions rather than captured by single threshold splits.

The categorical variables show more mixed behavior. The time signature dummy X0 ranks unusually high in logistic regression and Lasso, reflecting sensitivity to sparse categories with high leverage. However, it does not appear consistently in the top ranks of tree-based models, where its contribution is either minimal or absent. Mode major appears only weakly in XGBoost and is otherwise not prominent. Overall, these patterns suggest that categorical structural features contribute limited stable signal, and their importance in linear models is likely driven by rare but influential observations rather than robust aesthetic structure.

## 10.3 Lasso Feature Retention Compared to Logistic Regression

```{r lasso-retention}
lr_top    <- lr_imp_df    %>% arrange(desc(importance)) %>% pull(feature)
lasso_top <- lasso_imp_df %>% arrange(desc(importance)) %>% pull(feature)

cat("Features retained by Lasso:", length(lasso_top), "\n")
cat("Features dropped by Lasso :",
    length(lr_top) - length(lasso_top), "\n")

cat("\nFeatures in Lasso top 10 not in LR top 10:\n")
lasso_not_lr <- setdiff(head(lasso_top, 10), head(lr_top, 10))
if (length(lasso_not_lr) == 0) cat("  (none)\n") else
  cat(" ", paste(lasso_not_lr, collapse = ", "), "\n")

cat("\nFeatures in LR top 10 not in Lasso top 10:\n")
lr_not_lasso <- setdiff(head(lr_top, 10), head(lasso_top, 10))
if (length(lr_not_lasso) == 0) cat("  (none)\n") else
  cat(" ", paste(lr_not_lasso, collapse = ", "), "\n")
```

The Lasso model retained 27 of 28 features after one hot encoding, removing exactly one feature. The complete alignment between the Lasso and logistic regression top 10, with no features appearing in one list but not the other, indicates that minimal regularization at lambda equal to 0.0001 did not meaningfully change the structure of feature importance. The dropped feature is therefore best interpreted as fully redundant, contributing no independent linear signal beyond what is already captured by the remaining predictors.

This result has two main implications. First, the linear signal in this dataset is distributed across almost all available features rather than concentrated in a small subset. This means that feature reduction through sparsity is not naturally supported by the data, and a simplified linear model would not easily emerge from regularization alone. Second, the identical top 10 structure across logistic regression and Lasso confirms that the large coefficient associated with time_signature_X0 in the unregularized model reflects scaling instability rather than unique predictive relevance. Lasso reduces this coefficient while preserving its presence in the model, indicating that the feature carries some signal but was previously exaggerated in magnitude due to correlation structure among predictors. The consistency of all other top ranked features across both models further confirms that the linear importance structure is stable and not sensitive to the presence or absence of regularization.

## 10.4 Super Learner Learner Weights

```{r sl-weights-summary}
cat("=== Super Learner Learner Weights ===\n\n")

sl_weights %>%
  mutate(out = paste0(
    "  ", str_pad(learner, 25, "right"),
    ": ", str_pad(weight, 7, "left"),
    "  (", model_type, ")"
  )) %>%
  pull(out) %>%
  cat(sep = "\n")

cat("\nAggregated weight by model family:\n")
sl_weights %>%
  group_by(model_type) %>%
  summarise(total_weight = round(sum(weight), 4), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(total_weight)) %>%
  mutate(out = paste0(
    "  ", str_pad(model_type, 25, "right"), ": ", total_weight
  )) %>%
  pull(out) %>%
  cat(sep = "\n")
```

The Super Learner weight table reports the output of the non-negative least-squares meta-learner after evaluating all nine candidate learners on their cross-validated out-of-fold predictions. The weights sum to one and represent each learner’s contribution to the final ensemble prediction. A weight of zero does not imply poor standalone performance, but rather that the learner does not add new information beyond what is already captured by other learners. A weight of one indicates that a single learner is sufficient to minimize cross-validated log-loss and that no combination with other learners improves performance.

SL.ranger receives a weight of exactly 1.0 and all remaining candidates receive 0. This is a decisive outcome rather than a marginal preference. The meta-learner evaluated all possible non-negative combinations of the nine candidate predictions and determined that assigning full weight to ranger alone produced the lowest cross-validated log-loss. This implies that ranger provides the best calibrated probability estimates among all candidates, consistent with its lowest cross-validated risk reported earlier. It also implies that the remaining learners do not contribute additional predictive information beyond what ranger already captures. In practical terms, any song correctly or incorrectly classified by another model is already handled the same way by ranger, leaving no residual improvement for the ensemble to exploit.

The zero weight on SL.xgboost is especially informative. XGBoost is a strong non-linear model with competitive individual performance, yet its predictions are highly aligned with ranger’s. Because of this overlap, it does not provide complementary signal that improves the ensemble once ranger is included. Both methods appear to learn similar interaction patterns among key audio features such as energy, acousticness, and instrumentalness, but ranger produces more reliable probability estimates in this setting.

The zero weights assigned to all linear and regularized linear models, including SL.glm and all SL.glmnet variants, indicate that purely linear combinations of features do not contribute additional predictive value once non-linear structure is available. The same applies to SL.gam, which captures smooth non-linear effects but not the higher-order interactions that tree-based methods can represent. SL.lda also receives zero weight due to its strong distributional assumptions and linear decision boundary, which are too restrictive for this classification task.

SL.mean also receives zero weight, which confirms that the ensemble is learning meaningful structure in the data. If none of the models contained signal, the baseline would typically retain weight, but here it contributes nothing once the other learners are included.

Taken together, the weight distribution shows that the predictive structure in this dataset is fully captured by a single non-linear tree-based learner. Adding other modeling families does not improve cross-validated performance, indicating that the dominant signal is interaction-driven rather than linear or smoothly additive.

## 10.5 Cross-Model Feature Agreement

```{r cross-model-agreement}
top10_each <- list(
  LR    = lr_imp_df    %>% slice_max(importance, n = 10) %>% pull(feature),
  Lasso = lasso_imp_df %>% slice_max(importance, n = 10) %>% pull(feature),
  DT    = dt_imp_df    %>% slice_max(importance, n = 10) %>% pull(feature),
  RF    = rf_imp_df    %>% slice_max(importance, n = 10) %>% pull(feature),
  XGB   = xgb_imp_df   %>% slice_max(importance, n = 10) %>% pull(feature)
)

agreed_all  <- Reduce(intersect, top10_each)
agreed_tree <- Reduce(intersect, list(top10_each$DT, top10_each$RF, top10_each$XGB))
agreed_lin  <- intersect(top10_each$LR,  top10_each$Lasso)

cat("=== Cross-Model Feature Agreement ===\n\n")

cat("In top 10 across all five models:\n")
if (length(agreed_all) == 0) cat("  (none)\n") else
  cat(" ", paste(agreed_all, collapse = ", "), "\n")

cat("\nIn top 10 for all tree-based models (DT + RF + XGB):\n")
if (length(agreed_tree) == 0) cat("  (none)\n") else
  cat(" ", paste(agreed_tree, collapse = ", "), "\n")

cat("\nIn top 10 for both linear models (LR + Lasso):\n")
if (length(agreed_lin) == 0) cat("  (none)\n") else
  cat(" ", paste(agreed_lin, collapse = ", "), "\n")
```

The cross-model feature agreement analysis identifies which features are credibly important regardless of modeling assumption. Because each model uses a different importance measure and a different inductive strategy, a feature that consistently appears in the top 10 across all five models cannot be an artifact of any single model’s structure. Its presence across all top 10 lists is the strongest model-agnostic evidence available in this analysis for which audio dimensions encode production aesthetic.

Seven features appear in the top 10 across all five models: energy, instrumentalness, speechiness, valence, danceability, liveness, and tempo. These seven form the core acoustic signature of the maximalist-alternative distinction as represented in Spotify audio features. Their agreement spans two linear models that assume an additive boundary, a single decision tree that learns rule-based splits, a random forest that aggregates many decorrelated trees, and a gradient boosting model that learns residual structure sequentially. A feature that is consistently important across all of these fundamentally different learning strategies is capturing a stable and robust signal that does not depend on any single modeling assumption.

The tree-based models agree on eight features, adding acousticness to the universal seven. This difference is directly interpretable. Acousticness is strongly negatively correlated with energy, and in linear models fitted jointly, the shared variance is absorbed primarily by energy, leaving acousticness with reduced apparent importance. Tree-based models do not combine features additively in the same way, so after energy is used in early splits, acousticness can still contribute additional separation in later splits. As a result, all tree-based models identify acousticness as important even though it does not consistently appear in the linear top 10. This indicates that acousticness should still be treated as part of the core feature set, with its importance masked in linear models by multicollinearity.

The linear models agree on ten features, adding duration_ms, time_signature_X0, and time_signature_X1.4 to the universal seven.
