These features appear only in the linear agreement set and are largely absent from tree-based top rankings. Duration contributes a steady directional effect that is easily captured in a linear coefficient but less useful for threshold-based splitting once stronger predictors are included. The time signature variables reflect sparse categorical effects that can produce large coefficients in logistic regression and Lasso due to leverage from rare categories, especially time_signature_X0, but do not provide stable splitting utility in tree-based models where such sparse events have limited impact on impurity reduction.

Taken together, the three agreement sets form a hierarchy of feature credibility. The core set of seven features represents the most robust and model-independent signal in the entire pipeline. Adding acousticness expands this core in a way that is suppressed only by linear modeling structure rather than absence of signal. The additional linear-only features represent weaker and more model-dependent effects that still carry predictive value but are less stable across different learning strategies. Any downstream interpretation or deployment should prioritize the universal seven features, with acousticness treated as an additional core signal supported by non-linear models.

## 10.6 Feature Importance Visualization

```{r importance-plot, fig.cap="Figure 10. Normalized feature importance for all five models.", fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
# Combine datasets and slice based on ABSOLUTE importance
importance_data <- bind_rows(lr_imp_df, lasso_imp_df, dt_imp_df, rf_imp_df, xgb_imp_df) %>%
  group_by(model) %>%
  # Determine top 10 using the absolute magnitude of the coefficient
  mutate(abs_imp = abs(importance)) %>%
  slice_max(abs_imp, n = 10, with_ties = FALSE) %>% 
  # Now normalize the original importance value for plotting
  mutate(importance = importance / max(abs(importance), na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    # FIX: Map raw model names to your specific factor level strings
    model = case_when(
      model == "Logistic Regression" ~ "Logistic Regression (|Coef|)",
      model == "Lasso"               ~ "Lasso (|Coef|)",
      model == "Decision Tree"       ~ "Decision Tree (Split Importance)",
      model == "Random Forest"       ~ "Random Forest (Permutation)",
      model == "XGBoost"             ~ "XGBoost (Gain)",
      TRUE                           ~ model
    ),
    feature = paste0(feature, "__", model),
    feature = reorder(feature, importance),
    model   = factor(model, levels = c(
      "Logistic Regression (|Coef|)", "Lasso (|Coef|)", "Decision Tree (Split Importance)",
      "Random Forest (Permutation)",  "XGBoost (Gain)"
    ))
  )

# Clean, raw string with standard newline escapes
subtitle_text <- "Linear models: color shows direction of effect.\nTree models: direction varies by feature value and interaction context."

# Drop the unused factor levels cleanly right before plotting
importance_data_clean <- importance_data %>%
  mutate(feature = factor(feature)) # This completely strips out the invisible placeholders!

# Plotting
ggplot(importance_data_clean, aes(x = feature, y = importance, fill = direction)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) sub("__.*", "", x)) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values   = c("steelblue", "coral"),
    na.value = "grey70",
    labels   = c("Alternative", "Maximalist", "Not applicable"),
    name     = "Direction"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~model, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) + 
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Top 10 Features by Model (Relative Importance)",
    subtitle = subtitle_text, 
    x        = NULL, 
    y        = "Relative Importance"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y     = element_text(size = 9.5),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(1.5, "lines"),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1.8, "lines")
  )
```

```{r sl-weights-plot, fig.cap="Figure 11. Super Learner candidate model weights."}
sl_weights %>%
  mutate(learner = fct_reorder(learner, weight)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = learner, y = weight, fill = model_type)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(weight, 3)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Model Family") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, max(sl_weights$weight) * 1.25)
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Super Learner: Contribution of Each Candidate Model",
    subtitle = paste("Weights reflect each learner's contribution",
                     "\nvia cross-validated stacking.",
                     "\nNot variable importance."),
    x = NULL, y = "Weight"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 10.7 Substantive Interpretation

Features that appear in the top 10 across all six models, regardless of whether the model assumes a linear boundary, learns threshold rules through recursive partitioning, or builds a non-linear ensemble, represent the most reliably important audio correlates of production aesthetic in this dataset. Seven features satisfy this criterion: energy, instrumentalness, speechiness, valence, danceability, liveness, and tempo. These features consistently carry signal across all modeling assumptions tested here, and their importance cannot be attributed to any single model’s inductive bias or importance metric. A feature that is consistently important for both logistic regression and gradient boosting is one whose relationship with the outcome is stable under both additive and interaction based representations of the feature space.

Acousticness occupies a distinct intermediate role. It appears in the top 10 of all three tree-based models but not in the two linear models, making it a feature whose importance is real but partially suppressed under linear assumptions. Its contribution depends on joint relationships in the feature space, especially its strong negative association with energy. Linear models absorb this shared variation primarily through the energy coefficient, which reduces the apparent contribution of acousticness. Tree-based models do not rely on additive structure in the same way and can recover its effect through sequential threshold splits, allowing acousticness to emerge as important once energy has already been accounted for. This supports treating acousticness as part of the core feature set for any non-linear modeling approach, even though it is not consistently ranked in linear models.

The Lasso retention results in Section 9.3 further clarify the structure of the linear signal. Lasso retained 27 of 28 features and produced an identical top 10 to logistic regression, indicating that regularization does not meaningfully alter which features carry linear signal. Rather than identifying a sparse subset of predictors, the linear structure is distributed across nearly all available features. This means the universally shared features are not the only relevant predictors in the linear setting, but they are the most stable and consistently influential ones. The remaining features still contribute additive signal, even if they do not consistently appear in top rankings.

The Super Learner weight results provide the most direct model level interpretation of these feature level findings. The assignment of full weight to SL.ranger and zero weight to all other learners, including linear models, XGBoost, GAM, and LDA, indicates that the non-linear tree based structure captures all predictive information available in the feature space. If linear and non-linear learners were capturing complementary aspects of the signal, the meta learner would assign positive weight to multiple model families. Instead, the all or nothing allocation indicates that the signal captured by linear models is fully contained within what the random forest already learns, rather than representing a separate source of structure.

The zero weight on SL.mean confirms that the feature set contains strong predictive signal beyond baseline prevalence, and the zero weight on SL.xgboost indicates that gradient boosting does not add information beyond what random forest already captures in this setting. Taken together, the feature level and model level results converge on a single interpretation. Production aesthetic is primarily determined by a non-linear interaction structure in the audio feature space, with energy and acousticness forming the central organizing axis and the remaining seven shared features providing secondary refinement. Random forest is the most appropriate model in this pipeline because it is the only candidate that fully captures this joint structure without requiring additive assumptions.

---

# 11 Conclusion

This project developed and evaluated a multi-model pipeline for classifying music production aesthetic, alternative versus maximalist, using Spotify audio features. Loudness was intentionally excluded to avoid circularity and to test whether the aesthetic distinction remains detectable beyond its most obvious proxy.

The exploratory analysis showed that acousticness and energy carry the strongest linear signal, consistent with the idea that maximalist production reflects compressed, high intensity sound while alternative production is more organic and less processed. Danceability and valence showed substantial overlap across classes, which motivated the use of models capable of capturing non-linear structure and feature interactions rather than relying on additive effects alone.

The performance gradient, from logistic regression through a decision tree and onward to random forest, XGBoost, and the Super Learner, shows how much predictive power comes from non-linearity. The gap between the linear models and the best tree based methods reflects signal that only appears when features are allowed to interact. The decision tree sits between the linear models and the ensemble methods, offering a small but clear improvement by introducing threshold based splits, while the ensemble methods capture substantially more structure.

The directional error analysis showed that the feature space is not symmetric in how it handles mistakes. Ambiguous songs are more often pushed toward the maximalist class than the alternative class, suggesting a structural tilt in how borderline observations are represented in the feature space.The error overlap analysis clarified that many models fail on the same subset of songs, but not all. A small group of tracks is consistently misclassified by every model, which sets a practical limit on how well this feature set can separate the two aesthetics. These cases likely reflect genuine aesthetic hybridity rather than model weakness.

The feature importance analysis identified the audio features that remain stable across all modeling approaches. Features that consistently appear in the top ranks across different importance definitions are the most reliable signals of production aesthetic, since they are not dependent on a single modeling assumption. The Super Learner weights provide a final summary at the model level. The ensemble assigns full weight to the random forest model, with other learners contributing no additional improvement in cross validated performance. This indicates that the non linear tree based structure captures the relevant signal in a way that linear and alternative non linear models do not meaningfully extend.

Taken together, the results suggest that production aesthetic is strongly encoded in audio features and is best captured through non linear decision structures. A streaming platform using this system would achieve performance close to the best ensemble models, with remaining errors concentrated in tracks that sit near the boundary between the two aesthetics.

---

# 12 Implementation Plan

Estimating a machine learning model is only the first step in moving from analysis to real use. A typical deployment workflow moves through several stages, starting from defining the practical use case, collecting and validating data, building an initial model, testing it in a prototype setting, and then deploying it with continuous monitoring and updates.

The final model used in this project, the Super Learner, is effectively equivalent to the random forest because it assigns all weight to that learner. In practice, this model can be packaged as a simple API using tools such as vetiver and plumber in R. A new song can be scored by sending its audio feature vector to the API, which returns a probability of maximalist production along with a binary classification based on a chosen threshold.

The threshold of 0.5 is used throughout this study for consistency, but it is not fixed in practice. Different applications may shift this threshold depending on whether they prioritize reducing false positives or false negatives. The directional error results in Section 8 show that alternative songs are more often misclassified as maximalist than the reverse, which suggests that some applications may benefit from raising the threshold above 0.5 when false maximalist predictions are more costly.

A key practical issue in deployment is the difference between the training data and real time data. The dataset used here is clean and structured, while live Spotify feature extraction may change over time as audio processing methods are updated. This creates a risk that incoming data may not follow the same distribution as the training data. For this reason, incoming features should be checked before prediction, including basic range validation and checks for unusual feature combinations that were not present in training. These safeguards help prevent silent failures where the model produces confident but unreliable predictions on out of distribution inputs.

Before full release, it is useful to run the system in a silent mode where predictions are generated but not shown to users. This allows monitoring of stability, detection of data pipeline issues, and adjustment of validation rules without affecting end users. In higher stakes settings, a controlled experiment can be used, where a portion of users receive recommendations based on the model while others receive standard recommendations. This allows direct measurement of whether the model improves user outcomes in practice.

There are also limits to how far this model can be applied across different cultural contexts. The labels used here are based on a producer network drawn mainly from Anglophone popular music. Applying the model to other musical traditions may impose this framework onto styles that follow different production norms, which can distort the interpretation of predictions. In those cases, the results should be treated as provisional and ideally validated against domain specific expertise or retrained using a labeling system grounded in the relevant musical context.

A related limitation is that the current binary structure may not capture new or emerging aesthetic categories. If new production styles develop over time, songs from these styles may be systematically misclassified because they do not fit the existing labels. This should not automatically be treated as model error, but rather as a signal that the underlying labeling system needs updating. Because the labels are derived from community structure in the producer network, the same method can be rerun on updated data to identify new clusters. This allows the classification system to evolve over time while maintaining a discrete category structure rather than forcing a continuous reformulation of aesthetic identity.

---

# 13 Monitoring and Recalibration Plan

Once the model is deployed, monitoring should run continuously across six core areas. These include the distribution of incoming input features, patterns of missing data, system usage over time, the distribution of predicted probabilities, predictive performance where new ground truth labels become available through periodic re-labeling using the producer network pipeline, and system level performance such as API latency and error rates. These signals are typically tracked using control charts based on statistical process control, which highlight shifts in mean and variability and flag situations where the system moves outside expected bounds.

Two main types of drift need to be tracked over time. Feature drift refers to changes in the distribution of input variables in new music compared to the training data. This can happen if production styles evolve or if Spotify updates how audio features are computed, leading to new combinations of values that were not present before. Feature drift does not always require immediate retraining, but it is an early warning signal that a new model should be trained in parallel while the current one is still in use. Calibration plots are often among the first indicators of this issue, since systematic overprediction or underprediction can appear before overall discrimination performance declines. Model drift refers to a direct decline in predictive performance, typically measured using metrics such as AUC and directional error rates on newly labeled data.

A more subtle issue is that the relationship between features and labels can change over time. This is not just a shift in input values, but a shift in meaning. The model learns associations such as high energy combined with low acousticness being linked to maximalist production. These associations reflect the historical period in which the training data was collected, and they are not fixed properties of music. As production styles evolve, the same feature patterns may come to represent different aesthetics. When this happens, the model continues to apply old associations to new contexts, leading to systematic errors on more recent music.

This type of change is different from feature drift because the input distribution may remain stable while the meaning of the features changes. It is often referred to as concept drift. Unlike feature drift, it cannot be detected reliably from input data alone and requires updated labels on new releases. If directional error rates increase for recent music while overall feature distributions remain stable, this is a strong indicator of concept drift rather than a data quality issue. In that case, retraining the model on updated labeled data is necessary, rather than simply recalibrating outputs.

There is also a producer level version of this issue. A producer may shift style over time, but the labeling system assigns a single static aesthetic label based on their position in the producer network. This means earlier works by that producer may be labeled in a way that no longer matches their later aesthetic direction. These cases introduce structured label noise that is tied to time rather than random error. Without a time aware labeling system, this cannot be fully corrected, so performance on older catalog entries from evolving artists should be interpreted carefully.

Monitoring also needs to account for the fact that the system itself can change the data it observes. If predictions are used to shape playlists or recommendations, then the distribution of user listening behavior will shift in response to those predictions. This makes it difficult to measure true model performance because the model is partially influencing the outcomes it is evaluated on. A practical solution is to maintain a small control group where predictions are not applied, allowing performance to be measured on data that is not affected by the system.

When drift is detected, there are two main responses. If the main issue is that predicted probabilities are no longer aligned with observed outcomes but ranking performance remains stable, then recalibration methods such as Platt scaling can be used. This adjusts the probability outputs without changing the underlying model. If ranking performance itself degrades, then a full retraining is required using updated data.

A structured governance plan is needed to manage these updates safely. This plan should define clear triggers for retraining, such as sustained drops in performance metrics or statistically significant shifts in feature distributions. It should also specify how models are validated before deployment and how data is separated to avoid leakage between training and evaluation. Finally, it should include an assessment of the real world impact of any changes, particularly in systems where predictions influence user experience and downstream decision making.

---

# References

Askin, N., & Mauskapf, M. (2017). What Makes Popular Culture Popular? Product Features and Optimal Differentiation in Music. American Sociological Review, 82(5), 910–944. https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122417728662

Asmitha, M., & Kavitha, C. R. (2026). Analyzing the Popularity of Soundscapes: Genre Classification and EDA Using Spotify. In A. Bhattacharya, S. Dutta, P. Dutta, & A. Rocha (Eds.), Data Mining and Information Security (Vol. 1387, pp. 149–175). Springer Nature Singapore. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-96-6060-5_12

Azerrad, M. (2002). Our band could be your life: Scenes from the American Indie underground 1981 - 1991 (1. Back Bay paperback ed). Little, Brown and Company.

Gracyk, T. (1996). Rhythm and noise: An aesthetics of rock. Duke university press.

Kim, K., & Askin, N. (2024). Feature-Based Structures of Opportunity: Genre Innovation in the American Popular Music Industry, 1958 to 2016. American Sociological Review, 89(3), 542–583. https://doi.org/10.1177/00031224241246271

Reynolds, S. (2006). Rip It up and Start Again: Postpunk 1978-1984. Penguin Publishing Group.

Seabrook, J. (2015). The song machine: Inside the hit factory (First edition). W. W. Norton & Company.

Vickers, E. (2010). The Loudness War: Background, Speculation and Recommendations.

Walser, R. (1993). Running with the Devil: Power, Gender, and Madness in Heavy Metal Music. University Press of New England.


---
